<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

**Hypothesis Testing** is a formal statistical procedure used to make decisions about population parameters based on sample evidence. It is a *confirmatory* data analysis method in which two competing statements about an unknown population parameter are evaluated using a calculated test statistic and a pre-specified probability threshold (significance level, **α**).

In the context of **Food Analysis**, hypothesis testing is the principal inferential tool used to determine whether observed differences between food samples (e.g., two brands of milk, two preservation treatments, a sample vs. a regulatory limit) are **statistically significant** or merely attributable to random sampling fluctuation.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Board Definition (Memorize Verbatim)**
> *"Hypothesis testing is a statistical methodology that enables the researcher to make an objective decision about the validity of a claim (hypothesis) concerning one or more population parameters, by using sample data, in order to draw reliable conclusions applicable to food quality, safety, and processing research."*

The two competing hypotheses are formally written as:

$$
H_0 : \text{Null Hypothesis (Statement of No Effect / No Difference / Status Quo)}
$$

$$
H_1 : \text{Alternative Hypothesis (Statement of Significant Effect / Difference / Research Claim)}
$$

| Symbol | Hypothesis | Food-Analysis Example (Fat % in milk) |
| :--- | :--- | :--- |
| $H_0$ | Null | $\mu = 3.5\%$ (label claim is correct) |
| $H_1$ | Alternative | $\mu \neq 3.5\%$ (label claim is wrong) |

---

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — The Courtroom Trial

Imagine a courtroom where a food company is being prosecuted for selling milk that does **not** match its labelled fat content.

- The judge does **not** declare the company guilty outright. Instead, the **default assumption** is that the company is *innocent* — the milk conforms to the label.
- This default assumption is the **Null Hypothesis ($H_0$)**: *The mean fat content equals the claimed value.*
- The prosecution must present **convincing statistical evidence** (the test statistic + p-value) to overturn this default.
- The **burden of proof** lies with rejecting $H_0$, not with accepting it.
- If the evidence is strong enough (p-value $< \alpha$, typically **α = 0.05** or **5%**), the judge *rejects* the default and rules for the alternative.
- If the evidence is weak, the judge *fails to reject* $H_0$ — which is **not** the same as proving $H_0$ true.

> [!NOTE]
> **Critical Terminology Distinction (Frequently Tested in KTU Exams):**
> We say **"Fail to Reject $H_0$"** — we **NEVER** say *"Accept $H_0$."*
> A failure to reject merely means the evidence was insufficient to overturn the default.

---

## 1.3 Why Hypothesis Testing Is Indispensable in Food Analysis

Food scientists routinely work with **samples**, not entire populations (e.g., they cannot test every grain of rice in a silo). Hypothesis testing bridges this gap by allowing **inference from sample to population** with a quantified, controlled risk of error.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Syllabus Highlight — Primary Use-Cases in Food Analysis:**
> 1. **Quality Control:** Testing whether a batch of biscuits matches the labelled mean weight or moisture content.
> 2. **Regulatory Compliance:** Verifying that pesticide residues, heavy metals, or microbial counts are below the **Maximum Residue Limit (MRL)** or **FSSAI / Codex** thresholds.
> 3. **Process Comparison:** Comparing mean vitamin retention between two drying techniques (e.g., tray drying vs. freeze drying).
> 4. **Adulteration Detection:** Testing whether added water in milk has shifted the freezing point significantly below the standard.
> 5. **Shelf-Life Studies:** Determining whether bacterial counts (CFU/g) exceed acceptable limits by day *N*.
> 6. **Sensory Evaluation:** Difference testing (Triangle test, Paired test) — underlying null is *"no perceivable difference."*
> 7. **New Product Development:** Validating that a reformulated product is *equivalent* to the original (Equivalence Testing).

---

## 1.4 The Significance Level (α) — The Decision Threshold

The **significance level α** is the maximum probability of making a wrong decision (rejecting a true $H_0$) that the researcher is willing to tolerate.

| α Value | Risk of Type I Error | Typical Use in Food Science |
| :--- | :--- | :--- |
| **0.10 (10%)** | High | Exploratory R&D, pilot studies |
| **0.05 (5%)** | Standard | **Default in most food-quality studies** |
| **0.01 (1%)** | Strict | Food safety decisions, regulatory limits, drug-residue testing |
| **0.001 (0.1%)** | Very strict | Confirmatory pathogen limits |

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The Six Logical Steps of Hypothesis Testing (Universal Procedure)

Every hypothesis test, regardless of the specific statistic used, follows this canonical six-step protocol. KTU examiners frequently award marks simply for correctly identifying and applying these steps.

1. **State the Hypotheses:** Define $H_0$ and $H_1$ in terms of the population parameter. Specify whether the test is **two-tailed**, **left-tailed**, or **right-tailed**.

2. **Choose the Significance Level (α):** Common choices are **α = 0.05** or **α = 0.01**. This is set *before* collecting data.

3. **Identify the Appropriate Test Statistic:** Select based on data type, sample size, and whether population parameters are known. The choice of test determines the underlying sampling distribution (Z, t, F, χ², etc.).

4. **Compute the Test Statistic from Sample Data:** Apply the formula to the observed sample mean(s), variance(s), and sample size(s).

5. **Determine the Critical Value / Rejection Region OR Compute the p-value:** Either compare the test statistic against a tabulated critical value, or compute the p-value and compare to α.

6. **Make a Decision and Interpret in Context:** State clearly: *"Reject $H_0$"* or *"Fail to reject $H_0$"* — and translate this into a food-science conclusion (e.g., *"There is sufficient evidence at the 5% level to conclude the mean fat content differs from the label claim."*)

> [!NOTE]
> **Two-Tailed vs. One-Tailed Tests — Selection Rule:**
> - If the research question asks *"Is there a difference?"* → **Two-tailed** ($H_1: \mu \neq \mu_0$). Critical region: $\alpha/2$ on each side.
> - If the research question asks *"Is the mean greater than X?"* → **Right-tailed** ($H_1: \mu > \mu_0$). Critical region: $\alpha$ on the right.
> - If the research question asks *"Is the mean less than X?"* (e.g., below a safety limit) → **Left-tailed** ($H_1: \mu < \mu_0$). Critical region: $\alpha$ on the left.

---

## 2.2 Types of Errors in Hypothesis Testing

Because the decision is made from a *sample*, there is an inherent risk of being wrong. The two types of error are formally defined as follows:

| Error Type | Definition | Probability | Food-Analogy Consequence |
| :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- |
| **Type I Error** | Rejecting $H_0$ when it is actually true (false positive) | $\alpha$ | Concluding a safe food is contaminated |
| **Type II Error** | Failing to reject $H_0$ when it is false (false negative) | $\beta$ | Concluding a contaminated food is safe |
| **Correct Decision (Power)** | Rejecting $H_0$ when it is false | $1 - \beta$ | Correctly detecting the contamination |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Trade-off Relationship:** Decreasing α makes Type I errors rarer **but** increases β (and decreases the **power = 1 − β**). The only way to simultaneously reduce both errors is to **increase the sample size n**, which is why replication is sacred in food analysis.

---

## 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet — Test Statistics (High-Yield Reference)

> **Critical Rendering Note:** All vertical bars denoting absolute value or condition are rendered as `\vert` or `\mid` so that the markdown table structure is preserved.

| # | Test Name | Test Statistic Formula | Sampling Distribution | Degrees of Freedom | Typical Food-Analysis Use |
| :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- |
| 1 | **One-Sample Z-Test** | $Z = \dfrac{\bar{x} - \mu_0}{\sigma / \sqrt{n}}$ | Standard Normal $N(0,1)$ | Not applicable | Comparing a sample mean to a known standard (σ known, n ≥ 30) |
| 2 | **One-Sample t-Test** | $t = \dfrac{\bar{x} - \mu_0}{s / \sqrt{n}}$ | Student's *t* | $\nu = n - 1$ | Comparing sample mean to a labelled/standard value (σ unknown) |
| 3 | **Two-Sample Independent t-Test** | $t = \dfrac{\bar{x}_1 - \bar{x}_2}{\sqrt{\dfrac{s_1^2}{n_1} + \dfrac{s_2^2}{n_2}}}$ | Student's *t* (approx.) | Welch's: $\nu$ via Welch–Satterthwaite eq. | Comparing two independent formulations (e.g., Brand A vs. Brand B) |
| 4 | **Pooled t-Test** (equal variances) | $t = \dfrac{\bar{x}_1 - \bar{x}_2}{s_p \sqrt{\dfrac{1}{n_1} + \dfrac{1}{n_2}}}$, $s_p^2 = \dfrac{(n_1-1)s_1^2 + (n_2-1)s_2^2}{n_1+n_2-2}$ | Student's *t* | $\nu = n_1 + n_2 - 2$ | Two independent samples with assumed equal variances |
| 5 | **Paired t-Test** | $t = \dfrac{\bar{d} - 0}{s_d / \sqrt{n}}$ | Student's *t* | $\nu = n - 1$ | Before/after treatment on *same* sample (e.g., vitamin C before/after cooking) |
| 6 | **One-Way ANOVA F-Test** | $F = \dfrac{MS_{Between}}{MS_{Within}}$ | F-distribution | $\nu_1 = k-1$, $\nu_2 = N-k$ | Comparing 3 or more means (e.g., 3 drying temperatures) |
| 7 | **Chi-Square Test for Variance** | $\chi^2 = \dfrac{(n-1)s^2}{\sigma_0^2}$ | $\chi^2$ | $\nu = n-1$ | Testing homogeneity / consistency of variability in a process |
| 8 | **Non-Parametric: Wilcoxon Signed-Rank** | Uses ranks of $\vert d_i \vert$ | Wilcoxon distribution | n | Non-normal paired data (small samples in sensory tests) |

> **Notation key:** $\bar{x}$ = sample mean; $\mu_0$ = hypothesised population mean; $\sigma$ = population standard deviation (known); $s$ = sample standard deviation (unbiased estimator); $n$ = sample size; $\bar{d}$ = mean of paired differences; $s_d$ = standard deviation of paired differences; $k$ = number of groups in ANOVA; $N$ = total observations.

---

## 2.4 The p-Value — Modern Decision Criterion

The **p-value** is the probability, *assuming $H_0$ is true*, of obtaining a test statistic at least as extreme as the one observed.

**Decision Rule using p-value:**
$$
\text{If } p \leq \alpha \implies \text{Reject } H_0
$$
$$
\text{If } p > \alpha \implies \text{Fail to reject } H_0
$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Misconception Alert (Common KTU Pitfall):**
> The p-value is **NOT** the probability that $H_0$ is true, and it is **NOT** the probability of making a Type II error. It is strictly the conditional probability of the *observed (or more extreme) data* given that $H_0$ is true.

---

## 2.5 Real-World Utility in Food Engineering

Hypothesis testing underpins:

- **HACCP validation:** Statistically verifying that a Critical Control Point reduces hazards below acceptable levels.
- **Shelf-life predictions:** Confirming that microbial growth follows a model and that a *Defined Shelf Life* is statistically supported.
- **Sensory discrimination tests:** Triangle, duo-trio, and paired-preference tests are all formal hypothesis tests with $H_0$: *"No sensory difference exists."*
- **Process capability studies:** Six-sigma-style capability indices ($C_p$, $C_{pk}$) test whether process variation is within specification limits.
- **Equivalence / non-inferiority testing:** Demonstrating a "clean-label" ingredient is statistically equivalent in functionality to the conventional one.

# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation of the One-Sample t-Test Statistic

We derive the test statistic from first principles so that the *meaning* of every term is transparent.

Let $X_1, X_2, \dots, X_n$ be a random sample drawn from a normal population with unknown mean $\mu$ and unknown variance $\sigma^2$. Define:

$$
\bar{X} = \frac{1}{n} \sum_{i=1}^{n} X_i \quad \text{(sample mean)}
$$

$$
S^2 = \frac{1}{n-1} \sum_{i=1}^{n} (X_i - \bar{X})^2 \quad \text{(unbiased sample variance)}
$$

By the Central Limit Theorem (for large n) and the definition of the Student's t-distribution, the pivot:

$$
T = \frac{\bar{X} - \mu}{S / \sqrt{n}}
$$

follows a **t-distribution** with $\nu = n - 1$ degrees of freedom, *provided* the underlying population is normal (or n is sufficiently large for the CLT to apply).

For testing $H_0: \mu = \mu_0$ versus $H_1: \mu \neq \mu_0$, the test statistic is therefore:

$$
t_{obs} = \frac{\bar{x} - \mu_0}{s / \sqrt{n}}
$$

Each component has a clear role:

- The numerator $\bar{x} - \mu_0$ is the **effect size** in original units (e.g., grams of fat per 100 mL).
- The denominator $s / \sqrt{n}$ is the **standard error of the mean** — a measure of sampling fluctuation.
- The ratio $t_{obs}$ therefore expresses *how many standard errors* the sample mean is from the hypothesised value.

---

## 3.2 Worked Example 1 — One-Sample t-Test (Mean vs. Label Claim)

**Problem Statement:**
A dairy brand labels its toned milk as containing **3.0% fat**. A quality-control lab tests a random sample of **n = 10** cartons and obtains the following fat percentages:

$$
\{2.8,\ 2.9,\ 3.1,\ 3.0,\ 2.7,\ 3.2,\ 2.9,\ 3.0,\ 3.1,\ 2.8\}
$$

At the **5% significance level**, test whether the true mean fat content differs from the label claim.

**Step 1 — Hypotheses:**

$$
H_0: \mu = 3.0\% \quad ; \quad H_1: \mu \neq 3.0\% \quad (\text{two-tailed})
$$

**Step 2 — Significance level:** $\alpha = 0.05$.

**Step 3 — Test statistic:** One-sample t-test (σ unknown, small sample). Degrees of freedom: $\nu = n - 1 = 9$.

**Step 4 — Compute the statistic:**

Sum of observations:

$$
\sum x_i = 2.8 + 2.9 + 3.1 + 3.0 + 2.7 + 3.2 + 2.9 + 3.0 + 3.1 + 2.8 = 29.5
$$

Sample mean:

$$
\bar{x} = \frac{29.5}{10} = 2.95\%
$$

Sum of squared deviations:

$$
\sum (x_i - \bar{x})^2 = (2.8-2.95)^2 + (2.9-2.95)^2 + (3.1-2.95)^2 + (3.0-2.95)^2 + (2.7-2.95)^2
$$
$$
+ (3.2-2.95)^2 + (2.9-2.95)^2 + (3.0-2.95)^2 + (3.1-2.95)^2 + (2.8-2.95)^2
$$

Evaluating each term:

$$
= 0.0225 + 0.0025 + 0.0225 + 0.0025 + 0.0625 + 0.0625 + 0.0025 + 0.0025 + 0.0225 + 0.0225
$$
$$
= 0.225
$$

Sample variance:

$$
s^2 = \frac{0.225}{10 - 1} = \frac{0.225}{9} = 0.025
$$

Sample standard deviation:

$$
s = \sqrt{0.025} \approx 0.1581\%
$$

Standard error of the mean:

$$
SE = \frac{s}{\sqrt{n}} = \frac{0.1581}{\sqrt{10}} \approx \frac{0.1581}{3.1623} \approx 0.0500\%
$$

Test statistic:

$$
t_{obs} = \frac{\bar{x} - \mu_0}{s/\sqrt{n}} = \frac{2.95 - 3.00}{0.0500} = \frac{-0.05}{0.0500} = -1.00
$$

**Step 5 — Critical value / p-value:**

For a two-tailed test with $\nu = 9$ and $\alpha = 0.05$:

$$
t_{critical} = t_{0.025,\ 9} = \pm 2.262
$$

**Step 6 — Decision and Conclusion:**

$$
\vert t_{obs} \vert = 1.00 < 2.262 = t_{critical}
$$

Since the observed test statistic falls **inside** the non-rejection region, we **fail to reject $H_0$**.

**Food-Science Interpretation:** *At the 5% level of significance, there is insufficient statistical evidence to conclude that the true mean fat content of the milk deviates from the label claim of 3.0%. The observed deviation of 0.05% can be reasonably attributed to random sampling variation.*

---

## 3.3 Worked Example 2 — Two-Sample Independent t-Test (Comparing Two Brands)

**Problem Statement:**
A food scientist wants to compare the **vitamin C retention (%)** in a fruit juice processed by two different pasteurisation methods.

- **Method A (High-Temperature Short-Time):** $\bar{x}_1 = 78.5\%$, $s_1 = 4.2\%$, $n_1 = 8$
- **Method B (Low-Temperature Long-Time):** $\bar{x}_2 = 72.0\%$, $s_2 = 3.8\%$, $n_2 = 8$

At $\alpha = 0.05$, test whether the mean vitamin C retention differs between the two methods. Assume equal variances.

**Step 1 — Hypotheses:**

$$
H_0: \mu_1 = \mu_2 \quad ; \quad H_1: \mu_1 \neq \mu_2
$$

**Step 2 — Significance level:** $\alpha = 0.05$.

**Step 3 — Test statistic:** Pooled two-sample t-test, $\nu = n_1 + n_2 - 2 = 14$.

**Step 4 — Compute:**

Pooled variance:

$$
s_p^2 = \frac{(n_1-1)s_1^2 + (n_2-1)s_2^2}{n_1 + n_2 - 2} = \frac{(7)(4.2^2) + (7)(3.8^2)}{14}
$$

Computing the squares:

$$
s_1^2 = 17.64, \quad s_2^2 = 14.44
$$

Substituting:

$$
s_p^2 = \frac{(7)(17.64) + (7)(14.44)}{14} = \frac{123.48 + 101.08}{14} = \frac{224.56}{14} = 16.04
$$

Pooled standard deviation:

$$
s_p = \sqrt{16.04} \approx 4.005
$$

Standard error of the difference:

$$
SE_{diff} = s_p \sqrt{\frac{1}{n_1} + \frac{1}{n_2}} = 4.005 \sqrt{\frac{1}{8} + \frac{1}{8}} = 4.005 \sqrt{0.25} = 4.005 \times 0.5 = 2.0025
$$

Test statistic:

$$
t_{obs} = \frac{\bar{x}_1 - \bar{x}_2}{SE_{diff}} = \frac{78.5 - 72.0}{2.0025} = \frac{6.5}{2.0025} \approx 3.246
$$

**Step 5 — Critical value:**

For two-tailed test with $\nu = 14$ and $\alpha = 0.05$:

$$
t_{critical} = t_{0.025,\ 14} = \pm 2.145
$$

**Step 6 — Decision and Conclusion:**

$$
t_{obs} = 3.246 > 2.145 = t_{critical}
$$

We **reject $H_0$**.

**Food-Science Interpretation:** *At the 5% level of significance, there is strong statistical evidence that the mean vitamin C retention differs between the two pasteurisation methods. The HTST method (Method A) appears to retain significantly more vitamin C than the LTLT method (Method B), suggesting HTST may be the superior preservation technique for this product.*

---

## 3.4 Analytical Decision Matrix — Choosing the Correct Test

This matrix is a high-frequency KTU exam differentiator. Use it to select the correct test based on the experimental design.

| Research Question | Number of Groups | Sample Size | σ Known? | Distribution | Recommended Test |
| :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- |
| Compare one mean to a standard | 1 | Small (n < 30) | No | Approx. Normal | **One-sample t** |
| Compare one mean to a standard | 1 | Large (n ≥ 30) | Yes | Normal | **Z-test** |
| Compare two independent means | 2 | Any | No | Normal | **Two-sample t (pooled or Welch)** |
| Compare paired observations | 2 (paired) | Any | No | Normal of differences | **Paired t-test** |
| Compare 3+ means | ≥ 3 | Any | No | Normal | **One-Way ANOVA (F-test)** |
| Compare variances | 1 or 2 | Any | No | Normal | **F-test or χ²-test** |
| Compare proportions / counts | ≥ 2 | Large expected freq. | N/A | Categorical | **Chi-square test** |
| Compare two medians (non-normal) | 2 | Small | No | Any | **Mann–Whitney U** |
| Compare paired medians (non-normal) | 2 (paired) | Small | No | Any | **Wilcoxon Signed-Rank** |

# SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Flowchart — The Universal Hypothesis-Testing Procedure

The following Mermaid flowchart captures the canonical six-step decision protocol. All node IDs are alphanumeric and free of reserved Mermaid keywords.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A1["Step 1: Define Research Question and Parameter of Interest"]
    A2["Step 2: State Null H0 and Alternative H1 Hypotheses"]
    A3["Step 3: Choose Significance Level alpha 0.05 default"]
    A4{"Step 4: Check Design n size sigma known distribution"}
    B1["Use Z test sigma known or n greater than 30"]
    B2["Use t test sigma unknown n less than 30"]
    B3["Use paired t test for matched samples"]
    B4["Use One Way ANOVA F test for 3 or more groups"]
    B5["Use Chi square test for categorical data"]
    C1["Step 5: Compute Test Statistic from Sample Data"]
    C2["Step 6a: Determine Critical Value from Statistical Table"]
    C3["Step 6b: Compute p value from sampling distribution"]
    D1{"Decision: Compare Test Statistic to Critical Value OR p to alpha"}
    E1["REJECT H0 Evidence supports H1 at level alpha"]
    E2["FAIL TO REJECT H0 Insufficient evidence against H0"]
    F1["Step 7: Interpret Decision in Food Science Context"]
    F2["Report p value effect size confidence interval"]

    A1 --> A2 --> A3 --> A4
    A4 --> B1 --> C1
    A4 --> B2 --> C1
    A4 --> B3 --> C1
    A4 --> B4 --> C1
    A4 --> B5 --> C1
    C1 --> C2
    C1 --> C3
    C2 --> D1
    C3 --> D1
    D1 -- Test Statistic in Rejection Region --> E1
    D1 -- Test Statistic in Non Rejection Region --> E2
    E1 --> F1
    E2 --> F1
    F1 --> F2
```

---

## 4.2 Block Diagram — Hypothesis-Testing Application Architecture in Food Analysis

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    P1["Raw Food Sample or Batch"]
    P2["Sample Preparation and Lab Analysis"]
    P3["Generate Quantitative Data Means Variances Proportions"]
    P4["State H0 and H1 in Food Science Terms"]
    P5["Select Test Based on Design and Data Type"]
    P6["Compute Test Statistic and p value"]
    P7{"Decision Rule p less than alpha"}
    P8["Reject H0 Conclusion in Food Context"]
    P9["Fail to Reject H0 Conclusion in Food Context"]
    P10["Quality Control Report Regulatory Submission"]

    P1 --> P2 --> P3 --> P4 --> P5 --> P6 --> P7
    P7 -- Yes --> P8 --> P10
    P7 -- No --> P9 --> P10
```

---

## 4.3 Block Diagram — Mapping Errors to Food-Safety Outcomes

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    R1["Reality: H0 is TRUE Clean Food or True Label Claim"]
    R2["Reality: H0 is FALSE Contaminated Food or Mislabeling"]
    D1["Decision: Reject H0"]
    D2["Decision: Fail to Reject H0"]
    O1["Type I Error Alpha False Alarm Unnecessary Recall"]
    O2["Correct Decision Power 1 minus beta"]
    O3["Correct Decision True Label Verified"]
    O4["Type II Error Beta Missed Detection Public Health Risk"]

    R1 --> D1 --> O1
    R1 --> D2 --> O3
    R2 --> D1 --> O2
    R2 --> D2 --> O4
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Interpretation of the Error Map:**
> The Type II error (missed detection) is generally far more costly in food safety than a Type I error, because contaminated food reaching consumers can cause illness or death. This is why **α = 0.01** (rather than 0.05) is often mandated in regulatory microbiological testing, and why sample sizes are deliberately inflated to boost statistical **power (1 − β)**.

# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

---

## PART A QUESTIONS (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1 [KTU University Exam – Model Question]
**Define hypothesis testing. Distinguish clearly between the null hypothesis ($H_0$) and the alternative hypothesis ($H_1$) with one food-analysis example for each.**

**Model Answer:**

Hypothesis testing is a statistical procedure for deciding, on the basis of sample data, whether a stated claim about one or more population parameters is tenable.

- **Null Hypothesis ($H_0$):** The default claim of *no effect, no difference, or status quo*. It is the hypothesis that is directly tested.
- **Alternative Hypothesis ($H_1$ or $H_a$):** The competing claim — what the researcher is trying to demonstrate.

**Food-analysis example:**
Let $\mu$ denote the true mean fat percentage in a brand of milk labelled as 3.0%.

$$
H_0: \mu = 3.0\% \quad \text{(the label claim is correct)}
$$
$$
H_1: \mu \neq 3.0\% \quad \text{(the label claim is incorrect)}
$$

The test is conducted at a pre-specified significance level $\alpha$ (commonly 0.05), and the decision is to either reject $H_0$ (evidence supports $H_1$) or fail to reject $H_0$ (insufficient evidence against $H_0$).

---

### Question 2 [KTU University Exam – Model Question]
**Explain the concepts of Type I and Type II errors in hypothesis testing. Why is the Type II error often considered more dangerous in food-safety testing?**

**Model Answer:**

A Type I error occurs when a *true* null hypothesis is rejected (probability = α). A Type II error occurs when a *false* null hypothesis is not rejected (probability = β).

| Error | Definition | Probability | Food-Safety Consequence |
| :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- |
| Type I | Rejecting a true $H_0$ | $\alpha$ | False alarm; recall of a safe batch |
| Type II | Failing to reject a false $H_0$ | $\beta$ | **Contaminated food reaches consumers** |

In food-safety contexts (e.g., testing for *Salmonella* or pesticide residues above the MRL), failing to detect a real contamination is a public-health risk and can lead to outbreaks, product recalls, and legal liability. A false alarm, while economically costly, is far less harmful than a missed detection. Hence α is typically set very small (e.g., 0.01) and sample size is increased to raise the **power (1 − β)**.

---

## PART B QUESTIONS (14 Marks Each — Internal Choice)

### Question A (Choice 1) [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023 Style]
**(a) [7 Marks]** State and describe the six logical steps involved in hypothesis testing. For each step, identify what decision or output is produced.

**(b) [7 Marks]** A food-processing company claims that the mean shelf life of its pasteurised milk is at least **15 days**. A random sample of **n = 16** cartons is tested and yields $\bar{x} = 14.2$ days with $s = 1.8$ days. At the 5% significance level, test the company's claim. State clearly the hypotheses, test statistic, critical value, and conclusion.

#### Model Solution to (a):

1. **State the hypotheses:** Formulate $H_0$ and $H_1$ in terms of the population parameter. Decide on a one- or two-tailed direction.
2. **Choose the significance level α:** Commonly 0.05 or 0.01; fixed *a priori*.
3. **Select the appropriate test statistic:** Determined by data type, sample size, and whether σ is known. Output: a sampling distribution (Z, t, F, χ²).
4. **Compute the test statistic:** Substitute the sample values into the formula. Output: a single numerical value $Z_{obs}$ or $t_{obs}$.
5. **Determine the critical value or p-value:** Output: a tabulated critical value, or the p-value from the sampling distribution.
6. **Make a decision and interpret in context:** Compare the test statistic to the critical value (or p to α). Output: a decision (*reject* or *fail to reject $H_0$*) translated into a domain-specific statement.

#### Model Solution to (b):

**Step 1 — Hypotheses:**

The claim is *"at least 15 days,"* which becomes the null (status quo). The alternative is the negation — the mean is less than 15 days:

$$
H_0: \mu \geq 15 \text{ days} \quad ; \quad H_1: \mu < 15 \text{ days} \quad (\text{left-tailed})
$$

**Step 2 — Significance level:** $\alpha = 0.05$.

**Step 3 — Test statistic:** One-sample t-test (σ unknown, n = 16, $\nu = n - 1 = 15$).

**Step 4 — Compute:**

$$
t_{obs} = \frac{\bar{x} - \mu_0}{s / \sqrt{n}} = \frac{14.2 - 15.0}{1.8 / \sqrt{16}} = \frac{-0.8}{1.8 / 4} = \frac{-0.8}{0.45} \approx -1.778
$$

**Step 5 — Critical value:**

For a left-tailed test with $\nu = 15$ and $\alpha = 0.05$:

$$
t_{critical} = -t_{0.05,\ 15} = -1.753
$$

**Step 6 — Decision and Conclusion:**

$$
t_{obs} = -1.778 < -1.753 = t_{critical}
$$

The observed test statistic falls within the rejection region, so we **reject $H_0$**.

**Food-Science Interpretation:** *At the 5% level of significance, there is sufficient evidence to conclude that the true mean shelf life of the pasteurised milk is **less than** 15 days. The company's claim is statistically unsupported. The quality-control team should investigate accelerated spoilage causes (e.g., post-pasteurisation contamination, sub-optimal storage temperature).*

---

### Question B (Choice 2) [KTU University Exam – June 2024 Style]
**(a) [7 Marks]** With the help of a clear diagram, explain Type I and Type II errors in hypothesis testing. Relate both errors to a real food-safety scenario of testing for the presence of a pesticide residue above the Maximum Residue Limit (MRL).

**(b) [7 Marks]** Two brands of fruit juice, Brand P (preservative added) and Brand Q (no preservative), are compared for their mean ascorbic acid (vitamin C) content. The following summary statistics are obtained from independent random samples:

- Brand P: $\bar{x}_P = 45.2$ mg/100 mL, $s_P = 3.1$ mg/100 mL, $n_P = 12$
- Brand Q: $\bar{x}_Q = 41.8$ mg/100 mL, $s_Q = 2.9$ mg/100 mL, $n_Q = 12$

Assuming equal variances, test at the 1% level of significance whether the mean vitamin C content differs between the two brands.

#### Model Solution to (a):

*(See Section 4.3 for the error-mapping diagram.)*

When testing whether the pesticide residue in a batch of apples exceeds the MRL, we set:

$$
H_0: \text{Residue} \leq \text{MRL} \quad ; \quad H_1: \text{Residue} > \text{MRL}
$$

- **Type I error (probability α):** Concluding the residue exceeds the MRL when it actually does not. Consequence: an unnecessary batch rejection, economic loss, but consumer safety is maintained.
- **Type II error (probability β):** Concluding the residue is within the MRL when it actually exceeds it. Consequence: **contaminated produce reaches consumers**, posing a potential health hazard and triggering regulatory action against the company.

Because the human-health cost of a Type II error is so severe in food-safety testing, regulatory agencies mandate small α (often 0.01) and large sample sizes to keep β small and power (1 − β) high (typically ≥ 0.80).

#### Model Solution to (b):

**Step 1 — Hypotheses:**

$$
H_0: \mu_P = \mu_Q \quad ; \quad H_1: \mu_P \neq \mu_Q \quad (\text{two-tailed})
$$

**Step 2 — Significance level:** $\alpha = 0.01$.

**Step 3 — Test statistic:** Pooled two-sample t-test, $\nu = n_P + n_Q - 2 = 22$.

**Step 4 — Compute the test statistic:**

Pooled variance:

$$
s_p^2 = \frac{(n_P - 1)s_P^2 + (n_Q - 1)s_Q^2}{n_P + n_Q - 2} = \frac{(11)(3.1^2) + (11)(2.9^2)}{22}
$$

Computing the squares:

$$
s_P^2 = 9.61, \quad s_Q^2 = 8.41
$$

Substituting:

$$
s_p^2 = \frac{(11)(9.61) + (11)(8.41)}{22} = \frac{105.71 + 92.51}{22} = \frac{198.22}{22} \approx 9.010
$$

Pooled standard deviation:

$$
s_p = \sqrt{9.010} \approx 3.002
$$

Standard error of the difference:

$$
SE_{diff} = s_p \sqrt{\frac{1}{n_P} + \frac{1}{n_Q}} = 3.002 \sqrt{\frac{1}{12} + \frac{1}{12}} = 3.002 \sqrt{0.1667} \approx 3.002 \times 0.4082 \approx 1.225
$$

Test statistic:

$$
t_{obs} = \frac{\bar{x}_P - \bar{x}_Q}{SE_{diff}} = \frac{45.2 - 41.8}{1.225} = \frac{3.4}{1.225} \approx 2.776
$$

**Step 5 — Critical value:**

For two-tailed test with $\nu = 22$ and $\alpha = 0.01$:

$$
t_{critical} = \pm t_{0.005,\ 22} = \pm 2.819
$$

**Step 6 — Decision and Conclusion:**

$$
t_{obs} = 2.776 < 2.819 = t_{critical}
$$

The observed test statistic falls **just outside** the rejection region. We **fail to reject $H_0$**.

**Food-Science Interpretation:** *At the 1% level of significance, there is insufficient statistical evidence to conclude that the mean vitamin C content of Brand P differs from that of Brand Q. The observed difference of 3.4 mg/100 mL, while practically relevant, can plausibly be attributed to sampling variability. (Note: at the more lenient 5% level, the same data would yield a rejection — a classic illustration of how the choice of α influences the conclusion.)*

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Pitfalls**
> 1. **Direction of $H_0$ and $H_1$:** Students frequently set $H_0$ as the *research claim* and $H_1$ as the default — this is **backwards**. $H_0$ is *always* the status-quo / no-effect statement.
> 2. **Two-tailed vs. one-tailed mismatch:** If the alternative uses $\neq$, the critical value must be $t_{\alpha/2}$, not $t_{\alpha}$. Students often forget to halve α for two-tailed tests.
> 3. **Degrees of freedom:** Confusing $\nu = n$ with $\nu = n - 1$ for the one-sample t-test; or computing $\nu = n_1 + n_2 - 1$ instead of $n_1 + n_2 - 2$ for the pooled t-test. **Each error costs 1–2 marks.**
> 4. **Conclusion wording:** Writing *"accept $H_0$"* instead of *"fail to reject $H_0$"* is a **1-mark deduction** in most valuation keys.
> 5. **Units in interpretation:** Always state the conclusion with the original units (mg/100 mL, days, %, etc.) — a bare statistical conclusion without context is incomplete.
> 6. **p-value misinterpretation:** Do **not** state that p-value is the probability $H_0$ is true. State it correctly as the conditional probability of observing the data (or more extreme) *given* $H_0$.

---

## Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid-Revision Checklist)

- **Definition:** Hypothesis testing is a six-step inferential procedure to decide between $H_0$ (status quo) and $H_1$ (research claim) using a sample statistic and a pre-set significance level α.
- **Two hypotheses, one decision:** Formulate $H_0$ and $H_1$ such that they are mutually exclusive and collectively exhaustive.
- **Never "accept" $H_0$:** The correct phrase is *"fail to reject $H_0$."* Failing to reject ≠ proving true.
- **Six universal steps:** State → α → Select test → Compute → Critical value / p → Decide and interpret.
- **Errors:** Type I = false positive (α); Type II = false negative (β); Power = 1 − β.
- **Choosing a test:** Driven by (i) number of groups, (ii) sample size, (iii) whether σ is known, (iv) data normality, (v) independence vs. pairing.
- **Tail direction:** $\neq$ → two-tailed (use $\alpha/2$); $>$ → right-tailed; $<$ → left-tailed.
- **Degrees of freedom memorisation:** One-sample t: $n-1$; Paired t: $n-1$; Pooled two-sample t: $n_1+n_2-2$; One-way ANOVA: $k-1$ and $N-k$.
- **p-value rule:** Reject $H_0$ iff $p \leq \alpha$. The p-value is *not* the probability that $H_0$ is true.
- **Food-analysis applications:** label-claim verification, regulatory compliance (MRL, FSSAI), process comparison, shelf-life validation, sensory discrimination, adulteration detection, and equivalence testing.
- **Practical vs. statistical significance:** A statistically significant result may not be practically meaningful, and vice-versa. Always combine p-values with effect sizes and confidence intervals.
- **Sample size is the master knob:** Increasing $n$ simultaneously reduces both $\alpha$ and $\beta$ (and increases power). Replicate, replicate, replicate.
- **Standard error formula:** $SE = s / \sqrt{n}$ — note the $\sqrt{n}$ in the denominator; quadrupling $n$ halves $SE$.
- **Always conclude in context:** End every test with a food-science sentence — never leave the conclusion as a bare *"reject $H_0$."*

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Steps Involved in Conducting Hypothesis Tests and Interpreting the Results
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEFTT521 - MODELLING & SIMULATION IN FOOD PROCESSING
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Steps Involved in Conducting Hypothesis Tests and Interpreting the Results

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

In the context of food process modelling and simulation, **Hypothesis Testing** is a rigorous statistical inferential procedure used to make evidence-based decisions about population parameters (e.g., true mean fat content, true variance of microbial counts, or true proportion of defective packets) based on information gathered from a random sample drawn from the population.

Formally, it is a two-claim framework:

- **Null Hypothesis ($H_0$):** The default assumption of "no effect," "no change," or "no difference." It represents the status quo of the process (e.g., the mean shelf-life of a pasteurized milk packet is **7 days** as claimed by the manufacturer).
- **Alternative Hypothesis ($H_1$ or $H_a$):** The research claim that contradicts $H_0$. It is what the food scientist wishes to establish (e.g., the mean shelf-life is *not* 7 days, or is *less than* 7 days).

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Highlight:** In food processing, hypothesis tests are most commonly applied for (i) validating process specifications (e.g., moisture, fat, protein, sugar content), (ii) comparing two food formulations, (iii) comparing the effectiveness of preservation methods, and (iv) sensory evaluation studies comparing mean scores.

## 1.2 The Intuitive Analogy — The "Food Court Inspector"

Imagine you are a **Food Safety Officer (FSO)** standing outside a restaurant that displays a board claiming *"Our samosas weigh exactly 50 g."* You are suspicious that they actually weigh less. You cannot weigh *every* samosa (the population), so you buy **30 random samosas** (your sample) and weigh them.

- **Hypothesis Testing is the courtroom trial of statistics.** You treat the restaurant's claim ($H_0$) as "innocent until proven guilty."
- You, the inspector, act as the data collector. The weight measurements of the 30 samosas are your *evidence*.
- A test statistic (z, t, $\chi^2$, F) is essentially a measure of **"how far"** your evidence deviates from the claim.
- The **significance level ($\alpha$)** is the strictness of the court. $\alpha = 0.05$ means: *"I will only convict (reject $H_0$) if the evidence is so extreme that it could happen by random chance in less than 5 out of 100 trials."*
- The **p-value** is the probability that a samosa would weigh as little as your sample did, *if the claim of 50 g were really true.* If this probability is tiny, you reject the claim.

> [!NOTE]
> **Crucial KTU Examiner's Note:** A hypothesis test can **never *prove* $H_0$ is true.** It can only provide *evidence against* it. Therefore, the correct statistical phrasing is **"Fail to Reject $H_0$"** — never "Accept $H_0$." This single distinction is a 2-mark trap in KTU exams.

## 1.3 Type I and Type II Errors — The Inspector's Two Mistakes

| Error Type | Food Processing Interpretation | Probability | KTU Keyword |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Type I Error** | Rejecting a true process specification (e.g., recalling a perfectly good batch) | $\alpha$ | Producer's Risk |
| **Type II Error** | Failing to reject a false specification (e.g., a contaminated batch passes QC) | $\beta$ | Consumer's Risk |
| **Power of Test** | Correctly detecting a defective process specification | $1 - \beta$ | Sensitivity |

**Standard values used in KTU problems:** $\alpha = 0.05$ (5%) and $\alpha = 0.01$ (1%).
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The Six Canonical Steps of Hypothesis Testing (KTU Exam Favourite)

Every KTU question on this topic expects you to write these six steps in order. Memorize the order.

1. **Step 1 — State the Hypotheses:** Formally declare $H_0$ and $H_1$ in terms of the population parameter $\theta$. Identify if it is a two-tailed, left-tailed, or right-tailed test.

2. **Step 2 — Select Significance Level ($\alpha$):** Usually given in the problem (commonly 0.05 or 0.01). This fixes the **critical region** and the **rejection region**.

3. **Step 3 — Choose the Test Statistic:** The choice depends on what is known (sample vs. population standard deviation, sample size, type of data).

4. **Step 4 — Compute the Test Statistic Value:** Plug the sample values into the formula.

5. **Step 5 — Determine the Critical Value / p-value:**
   - *Critical Value Approach:* Compare statistic to critical value.
   - *p-value Approach:* Compute p-value and compare to $\alpha$.

6. **Step 6 — Make the Decision and Interpret in Context:** Explicitly state whether to *reject* or *fail to reject* $H_0$, and translate this into a food-processing sentence.

## 2.2 KTU Formula Cheat Sheet — Tests for Means and Proportions

> [!IMPORTANT]
> All formulas below are extracted directly from the KTU-approved statistical reference material for PEFTT521. Master the **conditions of use** for each — the examiner loves asking *"Which test would you apply and why?"*

| Test | Formula for Test Statistic | When to Use in Food Processing | Degrees of Freedom |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Z-test (mean, $\sigma$ known)** | $Z = \dfrac{\bar{x} - \mu_0}{\sigma / \sqrt{n}}$ | Process is mature; historical $\sigma$ is known (e.g., moisture content of a well-established flour batch) | N/A |
| **t-test (mean, $\sigma$ unknown)** | $t = \dfrac{\bar{x} - \mu_0}{s / \sqrt{n}}$ | Pilot-scale R\&D; only sample standard deviation $s$ is available (e.g., new preservation trial) | $n - 1$ |
| **Z-test (proportion)** | $Z = \dfrac{\hat{p} - p_0}{\sqrt{p_0(1-p_0)/n}}$ | Attribute QC: defectives in packets (e.g., proportion of broken biscuits) | N/A |
| **$\chi^2$ (variance)** | $\chi^2 = \dfrac{(n-1)s^2}{\sigma_0^2}$ | Testing *consistency* of process variability (e.g., fill weight variance) | $n - 1$ |
| **Paired t-test** | $t = \dfrac{\bar{d} - 0}{s_d / \sqrt{n}}$ | Sensory evaluation (before vs. after a new flavor) on the same panelist | $n - 1$ |
| **Two-sample t-test** | $t = \dfrac{(\bar{x}_1 - \bar{x}_2) - 0}{s_p \sqrt{1/n_1 + 1/n_2}}$ | Comparing two formulations (e.g., fat content in brand A vs. brand B) | $n_1 + n_2 - 2$ |

> [!NOTE]
> The pooled standard deviation $s_p$ in the two-sample t-test is:
>
> $$s_p = \sqrt{\dfrac{(n_1-1)s_1^2 + (n_2-1)s_2^2}{n_1 + n_2 - 2}}$$

## 2.3 Critical Value Table (Most Used Values)

| Test Type | $\alpha = 0.05$ | $\alpha = 0.01$ |
|---|---|---|
| Two-tailed Z | $\pm 1.96$ | $\pm 2.576$ |
| Right-tailed Z | $+1.645$ | $+2.33$ |
| Left-tailed Z | $-1.645$ | $-2.33$ |
| Two-tailed t (df = 9) | $\pm 2.262$ | $\pm 3.250$ |
| Right-tailed t (df = 9) | $+1.833$ | $+2.821$ |
| $\chi^2$ right (df = 9) | $16.919$ | $21.666$ |

## 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

In food process modelling, hypothesis tests serve three engineering purposes:

1. **Process Validation (Confirmatory):** Verifying that the actual output of a process line conforms to the designed specifications (e.g., a 500 ml bottle actually contains between 495 ml and 505 ml).
2. **Comparative Studies (Exploratory):** Comparing the effect of two processing conditions (e.g., microwave vs. conventional baking on moisture retention).
3. **Quality Monitoring (Surveillance):** Periodic sampling of finished goods to ensure the population has not drifted from its certified mean.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Worked Example 1 — Z-Test (Mean, $\sigma$ Known) — Full Board Solution

> **Problem Statement (KTU-style):** A dairy plant packages toned milk. The label states the mean fat content is **4.0 g per 100 ml**. From long production history, the population standard deviation is known to be **$\sigma = 0.6$ g/100 ml**. A quality inspector draws a random sample of **$n = 36$** packets and finds the sample mean fat content to be **$\bar{x} = 3.8$ g/100 ml**. Test at the **5% significance level** whether the company's label claim is valid.

### Step 1 — State the Hypotheses

The company claims the mean is 4.0 g/100 ml. The inspector suspects it is different.

$$H_0: \mu = 4.0 \text{ g/100 ml (label claim is correct)}$$

$$H_1: \mu \neq 4.0 \text{ g/100 ml (label claim is not correct)}$$

This is a **two-tailed test** because we are testing for "different from," not specifically "less than" or "greater than."

### Step 2 — Significance Level

Given in the problem: $\alpha = 0.05$ (i.e., 5%). For a two-tailed test, we split $\alpha$ into two halves of 0.025 in each tail.

### Step 3 — Choose the Test Statistic

Since the population standard deviation $\sigma = 0.6$ is **known** and $n = 36 > 30$, we use the **Z-statistic**.

$$Z = \dfrac{\bar{x} - \mu_0}{\sigma / \sqrt{n}}$$

### Step 4 — Compute the Test Statistic

First compute the standard error of the mean:

$$\dfrac{\sigma}{\sqrt{n}} = \dfrac{0.6}{\sqrt{36}} = \dfrac{0.6}{6} = 0.1 \text{ g/100 ml}$$

Now compute the Z-value:

$$Z = \dfrac{3.8 - 4.0}{0.1} = \dfrac{-0.2}{0.1} = -2.00$$

### Step 5 — Determine the Critical Value

For a two-tailed Z-test at $\alpha = 0.05$, the critical values are:

$$Z_{\text{critical}} = \pm 1.96$$

### Step 6 — Decision and Interpretation

The **rejection region** is $Z < -1.96$ or $Z > +1.96$.

Our computed $Z = -2.00$. Since $-2.00 < -1.96$, the statistic lies **inside the rejection region**.

**Decision:** Reject $H_0$.

> [!NOTE]
> **Food Engineering Interpretation:** At the 5% significance level, there is sufficient statistical evidence to conclude that the mean fat content of the milk packets is *not* equal to 4.0 g/100 ml. The company should consider revising its label claim or recalibrating its fat-blending process.

## 3.2 Worked Example 2 — t-Test (Mean, $\sigma$ Unknown) — Full Board Solution

> **Problem Statement:** A food technologist claims that a new drying technique reduces the moisture content of mango pulp to **less than 2.5%** (the regulatory limit). A random sample of **$n = 10$** batches gives a sample mean of **$\bar{x} = 2.30\%$** and a sample standard deviation of **$s = 0.20\%$**. Test at the **1% significance level** whether the new technique achieves the target.

### Step 1 — State the Hypotheses

The claim is "less than 2.5%." This is a one-sided claim, so the alternative is directional.

$$H_0: \mu = 2.5\% \text{ (technique does not achieve the target)}$$

$$H_1: \mu < 2.5\% \text{ (technique achieves the target — moisture is reduced)}$$

This is a **left-tailed test**.

### Step 2 — Significance Level

$\alpha = 0.01$ (1%).

### Step 3 — Choose the Test Statistic

Since $\sigma$ is **unknown** and the sample size is small ($n = 10 < 30$), we use the **t-statistic** with degrees of freedom $df = n - 1 = 9$.

### Step 4 — Compute the Test Statistic

Standard error:

$$\dfrac{s}{\sqrt{n}} = \dfrac{0.20}{\sqrt{10}} = \dfrac{0.20}{3.1623} = 0.0632$$

t-value:

$$t = \dfrac{2.30 - 2.50}{0.0632} = \dfrac{-0.20}{0.0632} \approx -3.16$$

### Step 5 — Determine the Critical Value

For a left-tailed t-test at $\alpha = 0.01$ with $df = 9$:

$$t_{\text{critical}} = -2.821$$

### Step 6 — Decision and Interpretation

The **rejection region** is $t < -2.821$.

Our computed $t = -3.16$. Since $-3.16 < -2.821$, the statistic lies **inside the rejection region**.

**Decision:** Reject $H_0$.

> [!NOTE]
> **Food Engineering Interpretation:** At the 1% significance level, there is strong evidence that the new drying technique successfully reduces the moisture content of mango pulp to below 2.5%. The technologist's claim is statistically validated. The technique can be adopted at industrial scale, but pilot-to-plant validation (longer trials) is still recommended for shelf-life studies.

## 3.3 Worked Example 3 — p-Value Approach (Same Example 1 Recomputed)

Using Example 1 data ($Z = -2.00$, two-tailed test):

$$p\text{-value} = 2 \times P(Z \leq -2.00)$$

From the standard normal table:

$$P(Z \leq -2.00) = 0.0228$$

Therefore:

$$p\text{-value} = 2 \times 0.0228 = 0.0456$$

Since $p\text{-value} = 0.0456 < \alpha = 0.05$, we **reject $H_0$**. The conclusion matches the critical-value method. ✓

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Rule of Thumb for Interpretation:** "p-value is the smallest significance level at which we would reject $H_0$." A small p-value means the observed data is *inconsistent* with $H_0$.

## 3.4 Analytical Decision Matrix — Which Test Do I Apply?

| Question to Ask | If YES → Use | If NO → Move to Next Question |
|---|---|---|
| Is $\sigma$ known? | Z-test | — |
| Is $\sigma$ unknown and $n < 30$? | t-test | — |
| Are we testing a proportion/defect rate? | Z-test for proportion | — |
| Are we testing a variance/spread? | $\chi^2$ test | — |
| Are samples paired (same unit before/after)? | Paired t-test | Two-sample t-test |
| Are samples independent and $\sigma$ unknown? | Two-sample t-test | — |
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 The Hypothesis Testing Workflow (Mermaid Flowchart)

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Start: Define the food process problem] --> B[Step 1: State H0 and H1]
    B --> C{Two-tailed, Left-tailed, or Right-tailed?}
    C --> D[Step 2: Fix significance level alpha = 0.05 or 0.01]
    D --> E[Step 3: Choose the test statistic Z, t, chi-square]
    E --> F[Step 4: Compute test statistic from sample data]
    F --> G[Step 5a: Find critical value from table]
    F --> H[Step 5b: Compute p-value from distribution]
    G --> I{Test stat in rejection region?}
    H --> J{p-value less than alpha?}
    I -- Yes --> K[Step 6: Reject H0]
    I -- No --> L[Step 6: Fail to reject H0]
    J -- Yes --> K
    J -- No --> L
    K --> M[Interpret: Evidence supports the research claim]
    L --> N[Interpret: Insufficient evidence to support the claim]
    M --> O[End: Report decision in food process context]
    N --> O
```

## 4.2 Error-Decision Tree

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Reality of H0] --> B[H0 is actually TRUE]
    A --> C[H0 is actually FALSE]
    B --> D{Decision Based on Test}
    D -- Reject H0 --> E[Type I Error - alpha - Producer Risk - Recall a good batch]
    D -- Fail to reject H0 --> F[Correct Decision - Confidence 1 minus alpha]
    C --> G{Decision Based on Test}
    G -- Reject H0 --> H[Correct Decision - Power of test equals 1 minus beta]
    G -- Fail to reject H0 --> I[Type II Error - beta - Consumer Risk - Pass a bad batch]
```

## 4.3 Block-Level Functional Architecture of a Hypothesis Test in a Food QC Lab

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    P1[Raw Material Intake] --> P2[Define Specification mu0 sigma]
    P2 --> P3[Draw Random Sample n]
    P3 --> P4[Measure Attribute e.g. moisture]
    P4 --> P5[Compute Sample Statistic xbar and s]
    P5 --> P6[Compute Test Statistic Z or t]
    P6 --> P7[Compare with Critical Value from Table]
    P7 --> P8{Decision Block}
    P8 -- Reject --> P9[Flag for Process Adjustment]
    P8 -- Accept --> P10[Release Batch for Packaging]
    P9 --> P11[Feedback to Process Control Loop]
    P11 --> P2
```
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

> **[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023, Set A]**

**Q1.** Distinguish between a **null hypothesis** and an **alternative hypothesis**. Why can a hypothesis test never "prove" $H_0$ to be true?

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

The **null hypothesis ($H_0$)** is the statement of "no effect" or "no change" — it represents the existing belief, claim, or process specification (e.g., the mean fat content of milk = 4.0 g/100 ml). The **alternative hypothesis ($H_1$ or $H_a$)** is the statement that contradicts $H_0$ and is the claim the researcher is trying to establish (e.g., the mean fat content $\neq$ 4.0 g/100 ml). A hypothesis test cannot "prove" $H_0$ true because we work with sample evidence; absence of evidence against $H_0$ is not the same as evidence *for* $H_0$. The test can only quantify how unlikely the data is, assuming $H_0$ is true. The correct statistical decision is therefore **"Fail to Reject $H_0$"** and not "Accept $H_0$." **(3 marks)**

---

> **[KTU University Exam - May 2024, Set B]**

**Q2.** Define **Type I** and **Type II errors** in the context of food quality control. Which one is called the **producer's risk** and why?

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

A **Type I error** occurs when a true null hypothesis is incorrectly rejected — for example, a perfectly good batch of biscuits is recalled because the sample mean weight was found to be statistically different from the specification. A **Type II error** occurs when a false null hypothesis is incorrectly accepted — for example, a substandard batch with low protein content passes quality inspection. Type I error is called the **producer's risk** because it harms the manufacturer (unnecessary rejection of good products, wasted raw material, downtime). Type II error is called the **consumer's risk** because the buyer receives a defective product. The probability of a Type I error is fixed at $\alpha$, while the probability of a Type II error is $\beta$. **(3 marks)**

---

## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each) — Internal Choice Format

> **[KTU University Exam - May 2024, Module 1, Q1]**

### Question A (14 Marks)

A bakery produces cookies with a labelled mean weight of **50 g**. From historical data, the population standard deviation is **$\sigma = 2$ g**. A random sample of **$n = 25$** cookies was taken and the sample mean weight was found to be **$\bar{x} = 49.2$ g**.

**(a)** Formulate the null and alternative hypotheses and identify whether it is a one-tailed or two-tailed test. **(3 marks)**

**(b)** Test at the **5% level of significance** whether the true mean weight of the cookies is significantly different from 50 g. Use the critical value approach. **(7 marks)**

**(c)** Compute the **p-value** for the test and verify your decision using the p-value approach. **(4 marks)**

#### Model Solution for Question A

**(a) Hypotheses Formulation (3 marks):**

The label claim is that the mean weight is 50 g. The quality team is checking for *any* deviation, so the test is two-tailed.

$$H_0: \mu = 50 \text{ g (label claim is correct)}$$

$$H_1: \mu \neq 50 \text{ g (label claim is not correct)}$$

This is a **two-tailed test**. **(3 marks)**

**(b) Critical Value Approach (7 marks):**

Given data:
- $\mu_0 = 50$ g, $\sigma = 2$ g, $n = 25$, $\bar{x} = 49.2$ g, $\alpha = 0.05$.

Since $\sigma$ is known, we use the Z-statistic:

$$Z = \dfrac{\bar{x} - \mu_0}{\sigma / \sqrt{n}} = \dfrac{49.2 - 50}{2 / \sqrt{25}} = \dfrac{-0.8}{2/5} = \dfrac{-0.8}{0.4} = -2.00$$

For a two-tailed Z-test at $\alpha = 0.05$, the critical values are:

$$Z_{\text{critical}} = \pm 1.96$$

The **rejection region** is $Z < -1.96$ or $Z > +1.96$.

Since $Z = -2.00 < -1.96$, the test statistic falls in the rejection region.

**Decision:** **Reject $H_0$** at the 5% level of significance. **(7 marks — 3 for computing Z, 2 for critical value, 2 for decision)**

**(c) p-value Approach (4 marks):**

For a two-tailed test:

$$p\text{-value} = 2 \times P(Z \leq -2.00) = 2 \times 0.0228 = 0.0456$$

Since $p\text{-value} = 0.0456 < \alpha = 0.05$, we **reject $H_0$**.

The decision from the p-value method **matches** the critical value approach. Both methods lead to the same conclusion. **(4 marks — 2 for calculation, 2 for decision)**

**Engineering Interpretation:** There is sufficient evidence at the 5% level to conclude that the true mean weight of cookies is not 50 g. The bakery should investigate the dough-cutting or portioning machine for calibration drift.

---

### Question B (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice

A new thermal preservation technique is claimed to reduce the average moisture content of a fruit bar to **less than 18%**. A sample of **$n = 9$** fruit bars was tested, yielding a sample mean of **$\bar{x} = 16.8\%$** and a sample standard deviation of **$s = 1.5\%$**.

**(a)** State the hypotheses and justify the choice of a **t-test** for this problem. **(3 marks)**

**(b)** Perform the hypothesis test at the **1% significance level** using the critical value approach. State clearly whether the new technique is statistically validated. **(7 marks)**

**(c)** If the same test were conducted at the **5% significance level**, would the conclusion change? Justify. **(4 marks)**

#### Model Solution for Question B

**(a) Hypotheses and Test Choice (3 marks):**

$$H_0: \mu = 18\% \text{ (technique does NOT achieve the target)}$$

$$H_1: \mu < 18\% \text{ (technique DOES achieve the target — left-tailed test)}$$

Justification: The population standard deviation $\sigma$ is **unknown**; only the sample standard deviation $s = 1.5\%$ is available. Also, $n = 9 < 30$, so the Central Limit Theorem does not apply. Therefore, the appropriate test is the **one-sample t-test** with $df = n - 1 = 8$. **(3 marks)**

**(b) Hypothesis Test at $\alpha = 0.01$ (7 marks):**

Standard error:

$$\dfrac{s}{\sqrt{n}} = \dfrac{1.5}{\sqrt{9}} = \dfrac{1.5}{3} = 0.5$$

t-statistic:

$$t = \dfrac{\bar{x} - \mu_0}{s / \sqrt{n}} = \dfrac{16.8 - 18.0}{0.5} = \dfrac{-1.2}{0.5} = -2.40$$

For a **left-tailed t-test** at $\alpha = 0.01$ with $df = 8$, the critical value is:

$$t_{\text{critical}} = -2.896$$

The **rejection region** is $t < -2.896$.

Since $t = -2.40 > -2.896$, the statistic does **not** lie in the rejection region.

**Decision:** **Fail to reject $H_0$** at the 1% level of significance. The new technique is **not** statistically validated at this strict level. **(7 marks — 2 for t-value, 2 for critical value, 2 for decision, 1 for interpretation)**

**(c) Re-evaluation at $\alpha = 0.05$ (4 marks):**

For a left-tailed t-test at $\alpha = 0.05$ with $df = 8$:

$$t_{\text{critical}} = -1.860$$

Since $t = -2.40 < -1.860$, the statistic **now** lies in the rejection region.

**Decision at 5%:** **Reject $H_0$** — the new technique IS statistically validated at the 5% level.

**Conclusion Change:** Yes, the conclusion changes with the significance level. At the stricter 1% level, we required very strong evidence to validate the new technique, and our sample did not provide it. At the more lenient 5% level, the same evidence is deemed sufficient. This illustrates a fundamental KTU lesson: **the choice of $\alpha$ is a design decision, not a mathematical truth.** In food industry QC, $\alpha = 0.05$ is most commonly used. **(4 marks)**

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfall Callout — Where Students Lose Marks**
>
> 1. **Never write "Accept $H_0$"** — it is always **"Fail to Reject $H_0$"** (lose 1 mark per occurrence).
> 2. **Forgetting to mention one-tailed or two-tailed** when stating hypotheses (lose 0.5 mark).
> 3. **Using the wrong critical value** for the t-test (e.g., using df = 9 instead of $n - 1 = 9$; the formula $df = n - 1$ gives 9, but always *recompute* — common slip: $df = 10$).
> 4. **Not interpreting in the context of the food problem** — KTU requires a concluding sentence linking the statistical decision to the engineering process. A bare "Reject $H_0$" with no interpretation loses 2 marks.
> 5. **Confusing $Z_{\alpha/2}$ with $Z_{\alpha}$** — for two-tailed tests at $\alpha = 0.05$, the table lookup is for $\alpha/2 = 0.025$, giving $\pm 1.96$, **not** $\pm 1.645$.
> 6. **Units missing in the final answer** — always quote the statistic with its unit if applicable (e.g., g/100 ml).

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid Revision Checklist)

- [x] **Hypothesis testing is a 6-step procedure**: Hypothesize $\rightarrow$ Set $\alpha$ $\rightarrow$ Choose test $\rightarrow$ Compute statistic $\rightarrow$ Critical value/p-value $\rightarrow$ Decide and interpret.
- [x] **Never say "Accept $H_0$"** — always say **"Fail to Reject $H_0$."**
- [x] **Z-test** when $\sigma$ is known or $n \geq 30$; **t-test** when $\sigma$ is unknown and $n < 30$.
- [x] **Critical values to memorize:** $Z_{0.025} = 1.96$, $Z_{0.005} = 2.576$, $Z_{0.05} = 1.645$, $Z_{0.01} = 2.33$.
- [x] **Two-tailed test:** "Is the parameter *different from* $\mu_0$?" — rejection region in both tails.
- [x] **One-tailed (right):** "Is the parameter *greater than* $\mu_0$?" — rejection region in the upper tail only.
- [x] **One-tailed (left):** "Is the parameter *less than* $\mu_0$?" — rejection region in the lower tail only.
- [x] **p-value rule:** Reject $H_0$ if $p < \alpha$; do not reject if $p \geq \alpha$.
- [x] **Type I error** = Producer's risk (rejection of a good batch). Probability = $\alpha$.
- [x] **Type II error** = Consumer's risk (acceptance of a bad batch). Probability = $\beta$.
- [x] **Power of test** = $1 - \beta$ = ability to detect a *truly* defective batch.
- [x] **t-distribution** is symmetric and bell-shaped like the normal but has **heavier tails** — so critical values are *larger* in magnitude than Z critical values.
- [x] **Degrees of freedom** for one-sample t-test = $n - 1$; for two-sample t-test = $n_1 + n_2 - 2$.
- [x] **Standard error of the mean** = $\sigma/\sqrt{n}$ (Z) or $s/\sqrt{n}$ (t) — the denominator is **always $\sqrt{n}$**, not $n$.
- [x] **Always end with a food-engineering interpretation sentence** — this is the difference between a 7-mark and a 12-mark answer.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: R-Squared
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEFTT521 - MODELLING & SIMULATION IN FOOD PROCESSING
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** R-Squared

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **R-Squared (Coefficient of Determination, $R^2$):** A dimensionless statistical metric that quantifies the **proportion of variance** in the dependent (response) variable that can be explained by the independent (predictor) variable(s) in a regression model. It is the most widely used goodness-of-fit indicator in food process modelling.

Mathematically, R-squared represents the ratio of the explained sum of squares to the total sum of squares, expressed as:

$$R^2 = 1 - \frac{SS_{res}}{SS_{tot}} = \frac{SS_{reg}}{SS_{tot}}$$

Where:
- $SS_{res}$ = Sum of Squares of Residuals (unexplained variation)
- $SS_{tot}$ = Total Sum of Squares (total variation)
- $SS_{reg}$ = Regression Sum of Squares (explained variation)

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

> [!NOTE]
> **Real-World Analogy — The Exam Score Predictor:**
> Imagine a food scientist is trying to predict the **moisture content of dried mango slices** based on **drying time**. After plotting experimental data and fitting a line, R-squared tells us: *"Out of all the variability seen in moisture content, what percentage is genuinely explained by the drying time, and what percentage is just random noise or unexplained factors?"*
>
> - If $R^2 = 1.00$ → the model explains **100%** of the variation (perfect prediction, almost never in real food data).
> - If $R^2 = 0.85$ → **85%** of the variation in moisture content is explained by drying time; the remaining 15% comes from humidity, air velocity, sample geometry, etc.
> - If $R^2 = 0$ → the predictor explains **none** of the variation (model is useless).

### 1.3 Key Properties (Standard Metrics)

- **Range:** $0 \leq R^2 \leq 1$ (in simple linear regression; can be negative for some non-linear models).
- **Dimensionless:** No units — it is a pure ratio.
- **Non-decreasing:** Adding more predictor variables will **never decrease** $R^2$ (this is why **Adjusted $R^2$** is also used).
- **Standard threshold for food process models:** $R^2 \geq 0.80$ is generally considered acceptable; $R^2 \geq 0.90$ is considered a good fit.

### 1.4 Syllabus Context (Food Processing Relevance)

In food process modelling, R-squared is critical when fitting:
| Application | Model Type | Typical Acceptable $R^2$ |
|-------------|------------|-------------------------|
| Drying kinetics (Page, Henderson-Pabis, Midilli) | Non-linear regression | $\geq 0.95$ |
| Shelf life prediction | Linear / polynomial | $\geq 0.90$ |
| Texture vs. storage time | Linear regression | $\geq 0.85$ |
| Kinetic models (Arrhenius, zero/first order) | Non-linear | $\geq 0.90$ |
| Moisture sorption isotherms (GAB, BET) | Non-linear | $\geq 0.95$ |

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Theoretical Foundation — The Three Sums of Squares

The concept of R-squared is built on partitioning the total variability in observed data into two parts:

1. **Explained Variation ($SS_{reg}$):** Variability accounted for by the regression model.
2. **Unexplained Variation ($SS_{res}$):** Random error or variability not captured by the model.

The relationship is:

$$SS_{tot} = SS_{reg} + SS_{res}$$

This is the foundational identity of least-squares regression. R-squared simply asks: *"What fraction of the total variation is explained?"*

### 2.2 Step-by-Step Logic of R² Computation

1. **Collect experimental data pairs** $(x_i, y_i)$ for $i = 1, 2, \ldots, n$.
2. **Compute the mean** of the observed $y$ values: $\bar{y} = \frac{1}{n}\sum y_i$.
3. **Fit a regression model** (linear or non-linear) to obtain predicted values $\hat{y}_i$.
4. **Calculate residuals:** $e_i = y_i - \hat{y}_i$.
5. **Compute the three sums of squares.**
6. **Apply the R-squared formula.**

### 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The following table is **high-yield for KTU University Exams** — memorize these formulas.

| # | Formula | Description | Units |
|---|---------|-------------|-------|
| 1 | $SS_{tot} = \sum_{i=1}^{n}(y_i - \bar{y})^2$ | Total Sum of Squares (total variation) | $(y)^2$ |
| 2 | $SS_{res} = \sum_{i=1}^{n}(y_i - \hat{y}_i)^2$ | Residual Sum of Squares (error) | $(y)^2$ |
| 3 | $SS_{reg} = \sum_{i=1}^{n}(\hat{y}_i - \bar{y})^2$ | Regression Sum of Squares (explained) | $(y)^2$ |
| 4 | $R^2 = 1 - \frac{SS_{res}}{SS_{tot}}$ | **Primary R-squared formula** | Dimensionless |
| 5 | $R^2 = \frac{SS_{reg}}{SS_{tot}}$ | Equivalent alternate form | Dimensionless |
| 6 | $R^2 = 1 - \frac{\sum(y_i - \hat{y}_i)^2}{\sum(y_i - \bar{y})^2}$ | Expanded form | Dimensionless |
| 7 | $R^2_{adj} = 1 - \left[\frac{(1 - R^2)(n-1)}{n-k-1}\right]$ | Adjusted R² (penalizes extra predictors) | Dimensionless |
| 8 | $RMSE = \sqrt{\frac{SS_{res}}{n}}$ | Root Mean Square Error (companion metric) | Same as $y$ |
| 9 | $MBE = \frac{1}{n}\sum(y_i - \hat{y}_i)$ | Mean Bias Error (sign of systematic error) | Same as $y$ |
| 10 | $\chi^2 = \frac{\sum(y_i - \hat{y}_i)^2}{n-k}$ | Reduced Chi-square (used in thin-layer drying) | $(y)^2$ |

> Where $n$ = number of observations, $k$ = number of predictors, $p$ = number of model parameters.

### 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility in Food Processing

R-squared is the **gold-standard reporting metric** in food process modelling literature. It is used to:

- **Validate drying models** (Newton, Page, Henderson-Pabis, Logarithmic, Midilli-Kucuk) on experimental moisture-ratio vs. time data.
- **Compare competing kinetic models** for nutrient degradation (vitamin C loss, browning index).
- **Confirm shelf-life prediction equations** linking quality attribute decay to storage temperature (Q₁₀ models).
- **Justify sorption isotherm models** (BET, GAB, Oswin) fitting equilibrium moisture content vs. water activity.
- **Verify rheological models** (Power-law, Herschel-Bulkley) fitting shear stress vs. shear rate.

### 2.5 Adjusted R² — Why It Matters

> [!NOTE]
> **Critical KTU Concept:** Plain $R^2$ has a flaw — adding **any** predictor (even a random, meaningless one) will *always* increase $R^2$. This makes it misleading when comparing models with different numbers of parameters. The **Adjusted $R^2$** corrects for the number of predictors and only increases if the new variable genuinely improves the model.

$$R^2_{adj} = 1 - \left[\frac{(1 - R^2)(n-1)}{n-k-1}\right]$$

For food process models with many parameters (e.g., GAB isotherm has 3 parameters), reporting **both $R^2$ and $R^2_{adj}$** is best practice.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Exhaustive Derivation: R² from First Principles

We start with the fundamental identity of least-squares regression:

$$SS_{tot} = SS_{reg} + SS_{res}$$

**Step 1:** Define the total deviation (raw deviation from the mean):

$$y_i - \bar{y} = (y_i - \hat{y}_i) + (\hat{y}_i - \bar{y})$$

This says: *"Total deviation = Unexplained deviation (residual) + Explained deviation (model)."*

**Step 2:** Square both sides to get the sums of squares:

$$(y_i - \bar{y})^2 = (y_i - \hat{y}_i)^2 + (\hat{y}_i - \bar{y})^2 + 2(y_i - \hat{y}_i)(\hat{y}_i - \bar{y})$$

**Step 3:** Sum over all $i = 1$ to $n$:

$$\sum(y_i - \bar{y})^2 = \sum(y_i - \hat{y}_i)^2 + \sum(\hat{y}_i - \bar{y})^2 + 2\sum(y_i - \hat{y}_i)(\hat{y}_i - \bar{y})$$

**Step 4:** Apply the **orthogonality property** of least-squares regression:

$$\sum(y_i - \hat{y}_i)(\hat{y}_i - \bar{y}) = 0$$

This is the key mathematical property — the residuals are orthogonal to the fitted values, making the cross-term vanish. Therefore:

$$\sum(y_i - \bar{y})^2 = \sum(y_i - \hat{y}_i)^2 + \sum(\hat{y}_i - \bar{y})^2$$

**Step 5:** Rewrite in standard notation:

$$SS_{tot} = SS_{res} + SS_{reg}$$

**Step 6:** Solve for $SS_{reg}$:

$$SS_{reg} = SS_{tot} - SS_{res}$$

**Step 7:** Define R-squared as the ratio of explained variation to total variation:

$$R^2 = \frac{SS_{reg}}{SS_{tot}} = \frac{SS_{tot} - SS_{res}}{SS_{tot}} = 1 - \frac{SS_{res}}{SS_{tot}}$$

**Final General Form:**

$$\boxed{R^2 = 1 - \frac{\sum_{i=1}^{n}(y_i - \hat{y}_i)^2}{\sum_{i=1}^{n}(y_i - \bar{y})^2}}$$

### 3.2 Analytical Matrix — Worked Numerical Example (Food Drying Context)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Problem:** A food engineer studies the drying of apple slices. Experimental moisture ratio (MR) and drying time (t) data are given. Compute $R^2$ for a linear model $MR = a - b \cdot t$.

**Given Data:**

| Observation ($i$) | Time $t_i$ (hr) | Observed $MR_i$ | Predicted $\hat{MR}_i$ |
|-------------------|-----------------|-----------------|------------------------|
| 1 | 0 | 1.000 | 0.980 |
| 2 | 1 | 0.880 | 0.870 |
| 3 | 2 | 0.760 | 0.760 |
| 4 | 3 | 0.650 | 0.650 |
| 5 | 4 | 0.540 | 0.540 |

**Step 1 — Compute the mean of observed MR:**

$$\bar{MR} = \frac{1.000 + 0.880 + 0.760 + 0.650 + 0.540}{5} = \frac{3.830}{5} = 0.766$$

**Step 2 — Compute $SS_{tot}$ (Total variation):**

$$SS_{tot} = \sum(MR_i - \bar{MR})^2$$

| $i$ | $MR_i$ | $MR_i - \bar{MR}$ | $(MR_i - \bar{MR})^2$ |
|-----|--------|-------------------|----------------------|
| 1 | 1.000 | +0.234 | 0.05476 |
| 2 | 0.880 | +0.114 | 0.01299 |
| 3 | 0.760 | -0.006 | 0.00004 |
| 4 | 0.650 | -0.116 | 0.01346 |
| 5 | 0.540 | -0.226 | 0.05108 |

$$SS_{tot} = 0.05476 + 0.01299 + 0.00004 + 0.01346 + 0.05108 = 0.13233$$

**Step 3 — Compute $SS_{res}$ (Unexplained variation):**

$$SS_{res} = \sum(MR_i - \hat{MR}_i)^2$$

| $i$ | $MR_i$ | $\hat{MR}_i$ | $MR_i - \hat{MR}_i$ | $(MR_i - \hat{MR}_i)^2$ |
|-----|--------|-------------|--------------------|-----------------------|
| 1 | 1.000 | 0.980 | +0.020 | 0.00040 |
| 2 | 0.880 | 0.870 | +0.010 | 0.00010 |
| 3 | 0.760 | 0.760 | 0.000 | 0.00000 |
| 4 | 0.650 | 0.650 | 0.000 | 0.00000 |
| 5 | 0.540 | 0.540 | 0.000 | 0.00000 |

$$SS_{res} = 0.00040 + 0.00010 + 0 + 0 + 0 = 0.00050$$

**Step 4 — Compute R-squared:**

$$R^2 = 1 - \frac{SS_{res}}{SS_{tot}} = 1 - \frac{0.00050}{0.13233} = 1 - 0.00378 = 0.9962$$

**Step 5 — Interpretation:**

> [!NOTE]
> $R^2 = 0.9962$ means **99.62% of the variation** in moisture ratio is explained by drying time. This is an **excellent fit** — well above the $R^2 \geq 0.95$ threshold for thin-layer drying models. The drying engineer can confidently use this linear model to predict apple slice moisture ratio at any drying time within the studied range.

**Step 6 — Bonus: Compute Adjusted R² (with $k = 1$ predictor, $n = 5$):**

$$R^2_{adj} = 1 - \left[\frac{(1 - 0.9962)(5-1)}{5-1-1}\right] = 1 - \left[\frac{0.0038 \times 4}{3}\right] = 1 - 0.00507 = 0.9949$$

Both $R^2$ and $R^2_{adj}$ are very high and close to each other, confirming that the single predictor (time) is highly relevant and the model is not over-parameterized.

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Mermaid Block: Conceptual Flow of R² Computation

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Start: Collect Experimental Data y_i] --> B[Compute Mean of Observations y_bar]
    B --> C[Fit Regression Model to Obtain Predicted y_hat_i]
    C --> D[Compute Residuals e_i = y_i minus y_hat_i]
    D --> E[Calculate SS_res = Sum of e_i squared]
    B --> F[Calculate SS_tot = Sum of y_i minus y_bar squared]
    E --> G[Apply Formula R_squared = 1 minus SS_res over SS_tot]
    F --> G
    G --> H{Is R_squared greater than 0.95}
    H -- Yes --> I[Model Accepted: Excellent Fit]
    H -- No --> J{Is R_squared between 0.80 and 0.95}
    J -- Yes --> K[Model Acceptable: Moderate Fit]
    J -- No --> L[Model Rejected: Poor Fit, Try Alternative Model]
    I --> M[Report R_squared and Adjusted R_squared in Research]
    K --> M
    L --> N[Consider Non-Linear Model or Additional Predictors]
```

### 4.2 Mermaid Block: Block-Level Functional Architecture of Goodness-of-Fit Evaluation

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph INPUT[Data Acquisition Stage]
        A1[Experimental Runs in Food Process] --> A2[Raw Data Pairs: x_i and y_i]
    end
    subgraph PROCESSING[Statistical Computation Stage]
        A2 --> B1[Calculate Mean y_bar]
        A2 --> B2[Fit Regression Model]
        B2 --> B3[Generate Predictions y_hat_i]
        B1 --> C1[Compute SS_tot]
        B3 --> C2[Compute SS_res]
        C1 --> C3[R_squared Calculator]
        C2 --> C3
        B2 --> C4[Count Predictors k]
        C3 --> C5[Adjusted R_squared Calculator]
        C4 --> C5
    end
    subgraph OUTPUT[Model Validation Stage]
        C3 --> D1{Decision: Accept or Reject Model}
        C5 --> D1
        D1 -- Accept --> D2[Deploy Model for Food Process Simulation]
        D1 -- Reject --> D3[Iterate: Modify Model Structure]
        D3 --> B2
    end
```

### 4.3 Mermaid Block: Interpretation Hierarchy of R² Values

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    Start[Compute R_squared Value] --> Q1{What is the value}
    Q1 -- 0.99 to 1.00 --> L1[Excellent Fit: 99 to 100 percent variance explained]
    Q1 -- 0.95 to 0.99 --> L2[Very Good Fit: Acceptable for Thin Layer Drying Models]
    Q1 -- 0.90 to 0.95 --> L3[Good Fit: Acceptable for Most Food Kinetic Studies]
    Q1 -- 0.80 to 0.90 --> L4[Moderate Fit: Use with Caution, Report Limitations]
    Q1 -- 0.50 to 0.80 --> L5[Poor Fit: Model Needs Major Revision]
    Q1 -- Below 0.50 --> L6[Unacceptable: Reject Model Entirely]
    L1 --> End[Document in Research Report]
    L2 --> End
    L3 --> End
    L4 --> End
    L5 --> Rec[Recommend Non-Linear Alternative]
    L6 --> Rec
    Rec --> End
```

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

---

**Q1. [KTU University Exam – Model Paper 2024]**

**Define the coefficient of determination (R²) used in food process modelling. State its range and one limitation.**

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**

> **Definition (2 marks):** The coefficient of determination, $R^2$, is a dimensionless statistical measure that represents the proportion of the total variation in the observed dependent variable that is explained by the independent variable(s) through the regression model.
>
> **Mathematical form:** $R^2 = 1 - \frac{SS_{res}}{SS_{tot}}$
>
> **Range (0.5 marks):** $0 \leq R^2 \leq 1$ for simple linear regression.
>
> **Limitation (0.5 marks):** $R^2$ always increases (or remains constant) when more predictor variables are added, even if those predictors are irrelevant. This is why **Adjusted $R^2$** is preferred when comparing models with different numbers of parameters.

---

**Q2. [KTU University Exam – Model Paper 2024]**

**Distinguish between $SS_{tot}$, $SS_{reg}$, and $SS_{res}$ as used in the calculation of R-squared.**

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**

| Term | Full Form | Interpretation | Formula |
|------|-----------|----------------|---------|
| $SS_{tot}$ | Total Sum of Squares | Total variability in observed data around the mean | $\sum(y_i - \bar{y})^2$ |
| $SS_{reg}$ | Regression Sum of Squares | Variability explained by the regression model | $\sum(\hat{y}_i - \bar{y})^2$ |
| $SS_{res}$ | Residual Sum of Squares | Unexplained variability (random error) | $\sum(y_i - \hat{y}_i)^2$ |

**Key relationship:** $SS_{tot} = SS_{reg} + SS_{res}$ (1 mark for the relationship)

---

### 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each — Module Internal Choice)

---

### **Question A (14 Marks) — [KTU University Exam – Model Paper 2024]**

**(a)** Derive the mathematical expression for R-squared ($R^2$) starting from the identity $SS_{tot} = SS_{reg} + SS_{res}$. State clearly the meaning of each sum of squares. **(7 Marks)**

**(b)** In a shelf-life study of a packaged food product, the following data on quality score ($Q$) vs. storage days ($t$) were obtained. Compute $R^2$ for the linear model $Q = a - bt$ fitted as $Q = 9.8 - 0.45t$.

| Day ($t_i$) | 0 | 2 | 4 | 6 | 8 | 10 |
|-------------|---|---|---|---|---|----|
| Observed $Q_i$ | 9.85 | 8.95 | 7.90 | 7.00 | 6.10 | 5.05 |

**Comment on the goodness of fit.** **(7 Marks)**

---

**Model Solution:**

**(a) Derivation (7 Marks):**

**Step 1 (2 marks):** Define each sum of squares with physical meaning:

- $SS_{tot} = \sum_{i=1}^{n}(y_i - \bar{y})^2$ — Total variation in the observed response around its mean $\bar{y}$. It captures the **overall scatter** in raw experimental data.

- $SS_{reg} = \sum_{i=1}^{n}(\hat{y}_i - \bar{y})^2$ — Variation in the **predicted** values around the mean. It captures the variability that the regression model **explains**.

- $SS_{res} = \sum_{i=1}^{n}(y_i - \hat{y}_i)^2$ — Variation due to the **residual** (difference between observed and predicted). It captures **unexplained** variability or random error.

**Step 2 (2 marks):** State the partitioning identity:

$$SS_{tot} = SS_{reg} + SS_{res}$$

**Step 3 (3 marks):** Derive R²:

R-squared is defined as the ratio of explained variation to total variation:

$$R^2 = \frac{SS_{reg}}{SS_{tot}}$$

Substituting $SS_{reg} = SS_{tot} - SS_{res}$:

$$R^2 = \frac{SS_{tot} - SS_{res}}{SS_{tot}} = 1 - \frac{SS_{res}}{SS_{tot}}$$

**Final expression:**

$$\boxed{R^2 = 1 - \frac{\sum_{i=1}^{n}(y_i - \hat{y}_i)^2}{\sum_{i=1}^{n}(y_i - \bar{y})^2}}$$

---

**(b) Numerical Computation (7 Marks):**

**Step 1 (1 mark):** Compute the mean of observed $Q$:

$$\bar{Q} = \frac{9.85 + 8.95 + 7.90 + 7.00 + 6.10 + 5.05}{6} = \frac{44.85}{6} = 7.475$$

**Step 2 (1 mark):** Compute predicted $\hat{Q}_i$ using $\hat{Q} = 9.8 - 0.45t$:

| $t_i$ | Observed $Q_i$ | Predicted $\hat{Q}_i = 9.8 - 0.45t$ | Residual $Q_i - \hat{Q}_i$ |
|-------|----------------|--------------------------------------|-----------------------------|
| 0 | 9.85 | 9.80 | +0.05 |
| 2 | 8.95 | 8.90 | +0.05 |
| 4 | 7.90 | 8.00 | -0.10 |
| 6 | 7.00 | 7.10 | -0.10 |
| 8 | 6.10 | 6.20 | -0.10 |
| 10 | 5.05 | 5.30 | -0.25 |

**Step 3 (1 mark):** Compute $SS_{tot}$:

| $Q_i$ | $Q_i - \bar{Q}$ | $(Q_i - \bar{Q})^2$ |
|-------|-----------------|---------------------|
| 9.85 | +2.375 | 5.6406 |
| 8.95 | +1.475 | 2.1756 |
| 7.90 | +0.425 | 0.1806 |
| 7.00 | -0.475 | 0.2256 |
| 6.10 | -1.375 | 1.8906 |
| 5.05 | -2.425 | 5.8806 |

$$SS_{tot} = 5.6406 + 2.1756 + 0.1806 + 0.2256 + 1.8906 + 5.8806 = 15.9936$$

**Step 4 (1 mark):** Compute $SS_{res}$:

| Residual | $(Q_i - \hat{Q}_i)^2$ |
|----------|----------------------|
| +0.05 | 0.0025 |
| +0.05 | 0.0025 |
| -0.10 | 0.0100 |
| -0.10 | 0.0100 |
| -0.10 | 0.0100 |
| -0.25 | 0.0625 |

$$SS_{res} = 0.0025 + 0.0025 + 0.0100 + 0.0100 + 0.0100 + 0.0625 = 0.0975$$

**Step 5 (1 mark):** Compute $R^2$:

$$R^2 = 1 - \frac{SS_{res}}{SS_{tot}} = 1 - \frac{0.0975}{15.9936} = 1 - 0.00610 = 0.9939$$

**Step 6 (2 marks):** Comment on goodness of fit:

$R^2 = 0.9939$ means that **99.39%** of the variation in quality score is explained by storage time. Since $R^2 \geq 0.95$, the linear model is an **excellent fit** for predicting shelf-life quality decay. The model can be confidently used for shelf-life estimation, and the small residuals indicate that the data is highly linear in nature with minimal experimental error.

---

### **Question B (14 Marks — Alternative Choice) — [KTU University Exam – Model Paper 2024]**

**(a)** Explain the concept of **Adjusted R-squared** with its formula. Why is it preferred over plain $R^2$ when comparing food process models with different numbers of parameters? **(7 Marks)**

**(b)** For a moisture sorption study of biscuits at 25°C, the following data were obtained. Two models are fitted:
- **Model 1 (BET):** $M = \frac{0.045 \cdot a_w}{(1 - a_w)(1 + 4.5 \cdot a_w)}$
- **Model 2 (Linear):** $M = 0.05 + 0.18 \cdot a_w$

Compute $R^2$ for both models using the data below and state which model is better. **(7 Marks)**

| $a_w$ | 0.10 | 0.20 | 0.30 | 0.40 | 0.50 |
|-------|------|------|------|------|------|
| Observed $M$ | 0.018 | 0.030 | 0.040 | 0.052 | 0.062 |

---

**Model Solution:**

**(a) Adjusted R-squared Explanation (7 Marks):**
