- $260 \times 13.5 = 3510$; $475 \times 7 = 3325$; diff = $185$.
- $260 \times 475 = 123500$; $17400 \times 7 = 121800$; diff = $1700$.

$$\det = 4(9275) - 260(185) + 7(1700) = 37100 - 48100 + 11900 = 900$$

The inverse (cofactor matrix / determinant) is:

$$(\mathbf{X}^T\mathbf{X})^{-1} = \frac{1}{900}\begin{bmatrix}
(17400 \cdot 13.5 - 475^2) & -(260 \cdot 13.5 - 475 \cdot 7) & (260 \cdot 475 - 17400 \cdot 7) \\
-(260 \cdot 13.5 - 475 \cdot 7) & (4 \cdot 13.5 - 7^2) & -(4 \cdot 475 - 260 \cdot 7) \\
(260 \cdot 475 - 17400 \cdot 7) & -(4 \cdot 475 - 260 \cdot 7) & (4 \cdot 17400 - 260^2)
\end{bmatrix}$$

Computing each cofactor:

- $C_{11} = 9275$, $C_{12} = C_{21} = -185$, $C_{13} = C_{31} = 1700$
- $C_{22} = 4 \cdot 13.5 - 49 = 54 - 49 = 5$
- $C_{23} = C_{32} = -(4 \cdot 475 - 260 \cdot 7) = -(1900 - 1820) = -80$
- $C_{33} = 4 \cdot 17400 - 260^2 = 69600 - 67600 = 2000$

Therefore:

$$(\mathbf{X}^T\mathbf{X})^{-1} = \frac{1}{900}\begin{bmatrix} 9275 & -185 & 1700 \\ -185 & 5 & -80 \\ 1700 & -80 & 2000 \end{bmatrix}$$

Now:

$$\hat{\boldsymbol{\beta}} = (\mathbf{X}^T\mathbf{X})^{-1}\mathbf{X}^T\mathbf{Y} = \frac{1}{900}\begin{bmatrix} 9275 & -185 & 1700 \\ -185 & 5 & -80 \\ 1700 & -80 & 2000 \end{bmatrix}\begin{bmatrix} 80 \\ 5460 \\ 153 \end{bmatrix}$$

Computing each:

- $\hat{\beta}_0 = \frac{1}{900}[9275(80) + (-185)(5460) + 1700(153)] = \frac{1}{900}[742000 - 1010100 + 260100] = \frac{1}{900}[8000] = 8.89$

Wait: $742000 - 1010100 = -268100$; $-268100 + 260100 = -8000$. So $\hat{\beta}_0 = -8000/900 = -8.89$.

- $\hat{\beta}_1 = \frac{1}{900}[-185(80) + 5(5460) + (-80)(153)] = \frac{1}{900}[-14800 + 27300 - 12240] = \frac{1}{900}[260] = 0.289$

- $\hat{\beta}_2 = \frac{1}{900}[1700(80) + (-80)(5460) + 2000(153)] = \frac{1}{900}[136000 - 436800 + 306000] = \frac{1}{900}[5200] = 5.778$

**Fitted Regression Equation:**

$$\boxed{\hat{Y} = -8.89 + 0.289\, X_1 + 5.778\, X_2}$$

**Interpretation:**

- $\hat{\beta}_1 = 0.289$ %/h per °C: For every 1 °C increase in drying temperature (with air velocity held constant), the moisture removal rate increases by **0.289 %/h** on average.
- $\hat{\beta}_2 = 5.778$ %/h per m/s: For every 1 m/s increase in air velocity (with temperature held constant), the moisture removal rate increases by **5.778 %/h** on average.

**Prediction at $X_1 = 75, X_2 = 2.2$:**

$$\hat{Y} = -8.89 + 0.289(75) + 5.778(2.2) = -8.89 + 21.68 + 12.71 = 25.50\ \%/\text{h}$$

The predicted moisture removal rate is approximately **25.5 %/h**.

---

### ⭐ QUESTION B (14 Marks) — Alternative: MLR Goodness-of-Fit & Diagnostics

> **[KTU University Exam — June 2024, Supplementary]**
>
> A study was conducted to model the **L\* color value** (lightness) of fried chips as a function of **frying temperature ($X_1$, °C)** and **frying time ($X_2$, min)**. From $n = 6$ experimental runs, the following summary statistics were computed:
>
> - $\bar{Y} = 55.2$, $\sum Y_i = 331.2$
> - $SST = 240.0$, $SSE = 36.0$, $SSR = 204.0$
> - $k = 2$ predictors
>
> **(a)** Compute the **coefficient of determination ($R^2$)** and the **adjusted $R^2$ ($R^2_{adj}$)**. Comment on the model fit. **(7 marks)**
>
> **(b)** Compute the **overall F-statistic** and test the model significance at $\alpha = 0.05$ given $F_{0.05, 2, 3} = 9.55$. Also compute the **Mean Squared Error (MSE)** and the **standard error of regression ($s$)**, and interpret them in the context of color measurement. **(7 marks)**

#### Model Solution

**Part (a) — $R^2$ and Adjusted $R^2$ (7 marks):**

$$R^2 = 1 - \frac{SSE}{SST} = 1 - \frac{36.0}{240.0} = 1 - 0.15 = 0.85$$

The model explains **85%** of the total variation in the L\* color value of the fried chips.

$$R^2_{adj} = 1 - \frac{(1 - R^2)(n-1)}{n - k - 1} = 1 - \frac{(1 - 0.85)(6-1)}{6 - 2 - 1} = 1 - \frac{0.15 \times 5}{3} = 1 - \frac{0.75}{3} = 1 - 0.25 = 0.75$$

**Comment:** The model exhibits a strong fit. The adjusted $R^2$ (0.75) is lower than $R^2$ (0.85) because the penalty for having $k = 2$ predictors reduces the value, indicating that some of the $R^2$ gain is attributable to model complexity rather than genuine predictive power. The model is still acceptable, but the gap suggests that a simpler model (perhaps with only one predictor) might be considered. **(7 marks)**

**Part (b) — F-statistic, MSE, Standard Error (7 marks):**

**F-statistic calculation:**

$$F = \frac{SSR / k}{SSE / (n - k - 1)} = \frac{204.0 / 2}{36.0 / 3} = \frac{102.0}{12.0} = 8.50$$

**Hypothesis Test:**

- $H_0: \beta_1 = \beta_2 = 0$ (model is not significant)
- $H_1:$ at least one $\beta_j \neq 0$ (model is significant)

**Decision:** Since $F_{calculated} = 8.50 < F_{critical} = 9.55$ at $\alpha = 0.05$ with $(2, 3)$ degrees of freedom, we **fail to reject $H_0$** at the 5% significance level. This means the overall model is **not statistically significant** at the 5% level, although it is significant at the 10% level (where $F_{0.10, 2, 3} = 5.46$).

> [!NOTE]
> **Engineering Interpretation:** Although $R^2 = 0.85$ looks impressive, the F-test reveals that with only $n = 6$ observations, there is insufficient evidence to declare the model significant at the conventional 5% level. A larger sample size is recommended.

**Mean Squared Error:**

$$MSE = \frac{SSE}{n - k - 1} = \frac{36.0}{6 - 2 - 1} = \frac{36.0}{3} = 12.0\ (\text{L*})^2$$

**Standard Error of Regression:**

$$s = \sqrt{MSE} = \sqrt{12.0} = 3.464\ \text{L* units}$$

**Interpretation in Food Color Context:**

- The MSE = 12.0 (L\*)² represents the average squared deviation between observed and predicted color values.
- The standard error $s = 3.464$ L\* units means that the typical prediction error is approximately ±3.46 L\* units.
- In sensory color analysis, an L\* difference of 3.46 is **perceptually noticeable** to a trained panel, suggesting the model, while explaining 85% of variance, is not yet precise enough for high-accuracy color prediction. A refined model with interaction terms or additional predictors may be needed. **(7 marks)**

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid-Revision Checklist — Multiple Linear Regression (Module 2):**

### 🔑 Core Concepts
- MLR models $Y$ as a linear combination of $k \geq 2$ predictors plus an error term.
- The intercept $\beta_0$ represents $E[Y]$ when all $X_j = 0$.
- Partial regression coefficient $\beta_j$ = effect of $X_j$ with all other $X$'s held constant.

### 🔑 The Master Formula
- $\hat{\boldsymbol{\beta}} = (\mathbf{X}^T\mathbf{X})^{-1}\mathbf{X}^T\mathbf{Y}$ — OLS estimator.
- The design matrix $\mathbf{X}$ **must** include a column of 1's for the intercept.

### 🔑 Model Quality Metrics
- $R^2 = 1 - SSE/SST$ — fraction of variance explained (use cautiously with many predictors).
- $R^2_{adj}$ — penalizes model complexity; preferred when comparing models.
- F-statistic tests **overall** model significance: $F = (R^2/k) / ((1-R^2)/(n-k-1))$.
- Individual t-tests evaluate each $\beta_j$ separately.

### 🔑 Five Gauss-Markov Assumptions
1. Linearity in parameters.
2. Independence of errors.
3. Homoscedasticity (constant variance).
4. Normality of errors.
5. No (or low) multicollinearity — diagnose with **VIF**.

### 🔑 Common Pitfalls in KTU Exams
- ❌ Forgetting the intercept column of 1's.
- ❌ Confusing $R^2$ with $R^2_{adj}$.
- ❌ Failing to give units for $\beta_j$ and engineering interpretation.
- ❌ Not checking $n > k+1$ before inverting $\mathbf{X}^T\mathbf{X}$.
- ❌ Reporting a prediction without stating the predictor values.

### 🔑 Food Process Applications
- Drying kinetics modelling (temperature, velocity, humidity).
- Texture prediction (hardness, crispness from formulation).
- Shelf-life correlation (time, temperature, water activity).
- Baking & extrusion optimization.
- Sensory-instrumental correlation studies.

> [!WARNING]
> **Final Examiner's Tip:** KTU Module 2 problems almost always carry a 7+7 mark structure. Spend the first half of your answer on the **matrix setup and computation**, and reserve the second half for the **engineering interpretation**. Marks are split roughly **50:50** between procedure and interpretation — never skip either.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Overview of Food Process Modelling and Its Applications
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEFTT521 - MODELLING & SIMULATION IN FOOD PROCESSING
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Overview of Food Process Modelling and Its Applications

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

**Food Process Modelling** is the systematic development of mathematical representations (equations, algorithms, and computational structures) that describe the physical, chemical, biochemical, and microbiological transformations occurring in food materials during processing, storage, and distribution. It quantifies the dynamic relationships between **process variables** (temperature, time, pressure, humidity, concentration) and **product attributes** (moisture content, color, texture, microbial load, nutritional quality).

Under the **KTU 2024 Scheme**, food process modelling is positioned as the bridge between *fundamental transport phenomena* and *real industrial food operations*, enabling **predictive engineering** rather than purely empirical trial-and-error development.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Syllabus Highlight (Module 3 – Overview):**
> A model in food processing is a *simplified mathematical surrogate* of reality. It is **not** the system itself, but a tool that captures the dominant mechanisms (heat, mass, momentum transfer, reaction kinetics) governing a food process with sufficient accuracy for **design, optimization, and control** purposes.

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

Think of a food process model like a **weather forecasting system** for a food factory:

- Instead of predicting rain, the model predicts, for example, how the moisture content of a slice of mango will drop over 6 hours inside a tray dryer.
- The **real dryer** is the "atmosphere" — too complex to observe continuously inside.
- The **mathematical model** is the "satellite + computer" — it uses physics (drying kinetics, heat & mass transfer) plus a few measured inputs (initial moisture, air temperature) to forecast outcomes.
- The **experiment** is the "ground truth" used to validate the satellite.

Just as a weather model never perfectly matches reality, a food process model is an **approximation**. Its value lies in being *good enough* to make reliable engineering decisions.

## 1.3 Why Food Process Modelling Is Needed

> [!NOTE]
> **Three Engineering Drivers for Modelling:**
> 1. **Cost Reduction** – Eliminates exhaustive physical experiments by simulation.
> 2. **Process Optimization** – Identifies optimal temperature, time, and formulation windows.
> 3. **Scale-Up & Safety** – Predicts behavior when moving from lab (1 kg) to plant (1000 kg) scale, and assesses microbial/inactivation safety margins.

## 1.4 Physical Constants & Standard Metrics in Food Process Modelling

> [!NOTE]
> **Standard Units & Constants Used Throughout Module 3:**
> - **Time (t):** seconds [s] or hours [h]
> - **Moisture content (M or X):** kg water / kg dry solid [kg/kg, db] or [% wb]
> - **Temperature (T):** Kelvin [K] or Celsius [°C]
> - **Thermal diffusivity (α):** m²/s
> - **Mass diffusivity (D):** m²/s
> - **Universal gas constant (R):** 8.314 J/(mol·K)
> - **Water activity (a_w):** dimensionless, 0 ≤ a_w ≤ 1

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Classification of Food Process Models

Food process models are classified based on their **theoretical foundation** and **spatial treatment**. This taxonomy is a high-yield area for KTU Part A questions.

### A. Based on Theoretical Foundation

| Model Type | Basis | Advantages | Limitations | KTU Use Case |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| **Empirical (Black-box)** | Statistical fitting of experimental data (regression) | Simple, fast, no physics needed | No extrapolation, no physical insight | Quick correlation of quality vs. time |
| **Theoretical (White-box)** | First-principles physics + transport phenomena | Universal, extrapolable, mechanistic | Complex, many parameters | Drying, sterilization, freezing |
| **Semi-theoretical (Grey-box)** | Simplified physics + fitted parameters | Balance of accuracy & simplicity | Limited mechanistic depth | Thin-layer drying models (Page, Lewis) |

### B. Based on Spatial Treatment

- **Lumped Parameter Model:** Assumes uniform property throughout the food (e.g., well-mixed liquid in a pasteurizer). No spatial gradients.
- **Distributed Parameter Model:** Accounts for spatial variation (e.g., temperature profile inside a frozen food slab). Uses partial differential equations (PDEs).

## 2.2 Steps in Developing a Food Process Model (KTU High-Yield Flow)

The development of a model follows a structured **7-step engineering workflow**:

1. **Define the Process Objective** – What outcome is being predicted? (e.g., moisture loss in drying, microbial inactivation in pasteurization).
2. **Identify Governing Mechanisms** – Heat transfer, mass transfer, reaction kinetics, or combinations.
3. **State Assumptions** – E.g., constant air temperature, negligible shrinkage, one-dimensional heat conduction.
4. **Write Governing Equations** – Mass balance, energy balance, momentum balance, kinetic rate laws.
5. **Specify Initial & Boundary Conditions** – e.g., initial moisture content, surface temperature equals air temperature.
6. **Solve the Equations** – Analytical (separation of variables) or numerical (finite difference, finite element).
7. **Validate with Experiments** – Compare predicted vs. measured values using statistical metrics (R², RMSE, χ²).

## 2.3 Key Rate Equations Used in Food Process Modelling

These are the **core kinetic expressions** that recur across Module 3 topics:

### (i) Zero-Order Reaction Kinetics
Used when reaction rate is **independent of concentration** (e.g., color degradation at high a_w, enzymatic browning in some cases).

$$-\frac{dC}{dt} = k_0$$

where $k_0$ = zero-order rate constant (concentration/time), $C$ = quality attribute (e.g., color, vitamin).

### (ii) First-Order Reaction Kinetics
Used when rate is **proportional to concentration** (e.g., microbial inactivation, vitamin loss, moisture loss in many foods).

$$-\frac{dC}{dt} = k \cdot C$$

Integrated form:

$$C(t) = C_0 \cdot e^{-k \cdot t}$$

where $k$ = first-order rate constant (1/time), $C_0$ = initial value.

### (iii) Arrhenius Equation (Temperature Dependence)
Captures how reaction rate changes with temperature — **central to thermal processing**.

$$k = k_{ref} \cdot \exp\left[\frac{E_a}{R}\left(\frac{1}{T_{ref}} - \frac{1}{T}\right)\right]$$

where $E_a$ = activation energy (J/mol), $T$ = absolute temperature (K), $R$ = 8.314 J/(mol·K), $T_{ref}$ = reference temperature.

### (iv) Fick's Second Law of Diffusion (Mass Transfer)

$$\frac{\partial C}{\partial t} = D \cdot \frac{\partial^2 C}{\partial x^2}$$

where $D$ = mass diffusivity (m²/s), $x$ = spatial coordinate (m).

### (v) Fourier's Second Law of Heat Conduction

$$\frac{\partial T}{\partial t} = \alpha \cdot \frac{\partial^2 T}{\partial x^2}$$

where $\alpha$ = thermal diffusivity (m²/s).

## 2.4 KTU High-Yield Formula Cheat Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Master these equations for Part A & Part B problems:**

| # | Equation | Application | Key Parameters | Typical Units |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | $C(t) = C_0 e^{-kt}$ | First-order degradation | $k, t$ | $C$: units of attribute; $k$: 1/s or 1/h |
| 2 | $t_{1/2} = \ln(2)/k$ | Half-life | $k$ | time |
| 3 | $k = k_{ref} \exp\!\left[\frac{E_a}{R}\!\left(\frac{1}{T_{ref}}-\frac{1}{T}\right)\right]$ | Temperature effect | $E_a, R, T$ | $E_a$: J/mol; $T$: K |
| 4 | $D_{ref} = D_T \cdot 10^{(T_{ref}-T)/z}$ | Microbial $D$-value | $z$ | $z$: °C |
| 5 | $\frac{\partial T}{\partial t} = \alpha \frac{\partial^2 T}{\partial x^2}$ | Heat conduction in solid food | $\alpha$ | m²/s |
| 6 | $\frac{\partial M}{\partial t} = D_{eff} \frac{\partial^2 M}{\partial x^2}$ | Moisture diffusion | $D_{eff}$ | m²/s |
| 7 | $MR = e^{-kt}$ (Lewis/Nelder) | Thin-layer drying | $k$ | dimensionless |
| 8 | $MR = e^{-(kt)^n}$ (Page) | Thin-layer drying | $k, n$ | dimensionless |
| 9 | $F_0 = \int 10^{(T-121.1)/10} dt$ | Sterilization value | $T$ | minutes |
| 10 | $\text{Sterility} = \log(N_0/N)$ | Log reduction | $N$ | dimensionless |

> [!NOTE]
> **$MR$ (Moisture Ratio) is defined as:** $MR = \frac{M(t) - M_e}{M_0 - M_e}$, where $M_e$ = equilibrium moisture content, $M_0$ = initial moisture content.

## 2.5 Real-World Engineering Utility

Food process models are not academic exercises — they directly drive:

- **Retort Process Design:** Calculating sterilization value $F_0$ for canned foods to ensure *Clostridium botulinum* inactivation (12-log reduction).
- **Cold Chain Logistics:** Predicting shelf life of frozen foods using Arrhenius kinetics.
- **Spray Dryer Optimization:** Simulating droplet drying curves to set inlet/outlet temperatures and feed rates.
- **Baking & Extrusion:** Coupling heat transfer (Fourier) with reaction kinetics (starch gelatinization, Maillard browning) for product quality.
- **Modified Atmosphere Packaging (MAP):** Coupled mass transfer + microbial growth model to predict shelf life.

# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Examination Standard:** Every derivation must show the starting equation, the **integration step**, the application of **initial conditions**, and the final rearranged form. Skipping steps leads to mark deductions.

## 3.1 Derivation 1: First-Order Kinetic Model (Most Frequently Asked)

### Statement
Show that for a first-order degradation reaction $\frac{dC}{dt} = -kC$, the concentration at time $t$ is given by $C(t) = C_0 e^{-kt}$.

### Step-by-Step Derivation

**Step 1 — Write the governing differential equation:**

$$\frac{dC}{dt} = -k \cdot C$$

**Step 2 — Apply separation of variables (move all $C$ terms to LHS and $t$ terms to RHS):**

$$\frac{dC}{C} = -k \, dt$$

**Step 3 — Integrate both sides from initial state $(t=0, C=C_0)$ to arbitrary state $(t=t, C=C)$:**

$$\int_{C_0}^{C} \frac{dC}{C} = -k \int_{0}^{t} dt$$

**Step 4 — Evaluate the integrals (logarithmic on LHS, linear on RHS):**

$$\big[\ln(C)\big]_{C_0}^{C} = -k \cdot t$$

**Step 5 — Expand the definite integral on the LHS:**

$$\ln(C) - \ln(C_0) = -k \cdot t$$

**Step 6 — Combine the logarithms using $\ln(a) - \ln(b) = \ln(a/b)$:**

$$\ln\left(\frac{C}{C_0}\right) = -k \cdot t$$

**Step 7 — Exponentiate both sides to isolate $C$:**

$$\frac{C}{C_0} = e^{-k \cdot t}$$

**Step 8 — Multiply both sides by $C_0$ to obtain the final form:**

$$C(t) = C_0 \cdot e^{-k \cdot t}$$

### Step 9 — Interpretation (Required for Full Marks in KTU)

This exponential decay is the **signature of all first-order processes** in food engineering:

- Microbial inactivation: $N(t) = N_0 e^{-kt}$
- Vitamin retention: $V(t) = V_0 e^{-kt}$
- Moisture loss in some foods: $M(t) = M_e + (M_0 - M_e)e^{-kt}$
- Quality loss (color, texture, flavor)

## 3.2 Derivation 2: D-Value and Z-Value Relationship (Microbial Inactivation)

### Statement
Derive the relationship $D_{ref} = D_T \cdot 10^{(T_{ref}-T)/z}$ used in thermal processing.

### Step-by-Step Derivation

**Step 1 — Definition of D-value:** Time required at temperature $T$ for a **1-log (90%) reduction** in microbial population.

$$D_T = \frac{2.303}{k_T}$$

where $k_T$ is the first-order inactivation rate constant at temperature $T$.

**Step 2 — Definition of z-value:** Temperature change required for a **10-fold change** in $D_T$.

$$z = \frac{T_2 - T_1}{\log(D_{T_1}) - \log(D_{T_2})}$$

**Step 3 — Take the logarithm of $D_T$ definition:**

$$\log(D_T) = \log(2.303) - \log(k_T) = 0.3622 - \log(k_T)$$

**Step 4 — Apply the z-value definition rearranged (log-linear relationship):**

$$\log(D_T) = \log(D_{ref}) - \frac{T - T_{ref}}{z}$$

**Step 5 — Solve for $D_T$ by exponentiating base 10:**

$$D_T = D_{ref} \cdot 10^{-(T - T_{ref})/z}$$

**Step 6 — Rearrange by multiplying numerator and denominator by $-1$:**

$$D_T = D_{ref} \cdot 10^{(T_{ref} - T)/z}$$

**Step 7 — Equivalently, the rate constant relationship:**

$$k_T = k_{ref} \cdot 10^{(T - T_{ref})/z}$$

This is the **thermal death time (TDT) model**, foundational for retort sterilization calculations.

## 3.3 Derivation 3: Arrhenius Form of Temperature Dependence (Activation Energy)

### Statement
Show how the Arrhenius equation $k = A \, e^{-E_a/(RT)}$ leads to the two-point form used in food shelf-life studies.

### Step-by-Step Derivation

**Step 1 — Start with the standard Arrhenius equation:**

$$k = A \cdot \exp\!\left(-\frac{E_a}{R \cdot T}\right)$$

where $A$ = pre-exponential factor (1/time), $E_a$ = activation energy (J/mol), $R$ = 8.314 J/(mol·K), $T$ = absolute temperature (K).

**Step 2 — Write the equation for a reference temperature $T_{ref}$:**

$$k_{ref} = A \cdot \exp\!\left(-\frac{E_a}{R \cdot T_{ref}}\right)$$

**Step 3 — Take the natural logarithm of both equations:**

$$\ln(k) = \ln(A) - \frac{E_a}{R \cdot T}$$

$$\ln(k_{ref}) = \ln(A) - \frac{E_a}{R \cdot T_{ref}}$$

**Step 4 — Subtract the second equation from the first (eliminates $A$):**

$$\ln(k) - \ln(k_{ref}) = -\frac{E_a}{R \cdot T} + \frac{E_a}{R \cdot T_{ref}}$$

**Step 5 — Factor out $E_a/R$ and combine over a common denominator:**

$$\ln\!\left(\frac{k}{k_{ref}}\right) = \frac{E_a}{R}\left(\frac{1}{T_{ref}} - \frac{1}{T}\right)$$

**Step 6 — Exponentiate both sides to obtain the two-point Arrhenius form:**

$$\frac{k}{k_{ref}} = \exp\!\left[\frac{E_a}{R}\left(\frac{1}{T_{ref}} - \frac{1}{T}\right)\right]$$

**Step 7 — Solve for $k$:**

$$k = k_{ref} \cdot \exp\!\left[\frac{E_a}{R}\left(\frac{1}{T_{ref}} - \frac{1}{T}\right)\right]$$

### Step 8 — Linear Form (Used to Determine $E_a$ Experimentally)

In practice, $\ln(k)$ vs. $1/T$ gives a straight line:

$$\ln(k) = \ln(A) - \frac{E_a}{R} \cdot \frac{1}{T}$$

- **Slope** $= -E_a/R$  →  $E_a = -R \cdot \text{slope}$
- **Intercept** $= \ln(A)$

This is the **most common linearization** in accelerated shelf-life testing (ASLT) of foods.

## 3.4 Analytical Matrix: When to Use Which Model (Decision Aid)

| Process | Dominant Mechanism | Recommended Model Type | Key Equation |
|---|---|---|---|
| Pasteurization of milk | Microbial inactivation | First-order + Arrhenius | $\log(N/N_0) = -t/D_T$ |
| Hot air drying of grains | Moisture diffusion | Fick's 2nd law / Page | $MR = e^{-(kt)^n}$ |
| Vitamin C loss in juice | Chemical degradation | First-order + Arrhenius | $C = C_0 e^{-kt}$ |
| Freezing of fish | Heat conduction | Fourier's 2nd law | Plank's equation |
| Browning in bakery | Maillard reaction | Zero or first-order | $C = C_0 - k_0 t$ |
| Fermentation kinetics | Microbial growth | Monod / Logistic | $dX/dt = \mu_{max} X$ |
| Lipid oxidation | Autoxidation chain | First-order + Arrhenius | $PV = PV_0 e^{-kt}$ |

# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1
**[KTU University Exam – May 2024, Model Paper Style]**
**Q: Define a food process model. Differentiate between empirical and theoretical models with one example each.**

**Model Answer:**

A **food process model** is a mathematical representation of a real food processing operation that relates input variables (temperature, time, moisture) to output responses (quality, safety, yield).

| Aspect | Empirical Model | Theoretical Model |
|---|---|---|
| Basis | Experimental data fitting | First principles (physics, chemistry) |
| Parameters | Statistical coefficients | Physical properties (k, D, α, E_a) |
| Extrapolation | Poor / Risky | Good within physics range |
| Example | Quadratic regression of color vs. baking time | Fick's law for moisture diffusion in drying |
| Example equation | $Q = aT^2 + bT + c$ | $\partial M/\partial t = D_{eff} \, \partial^2 M/\partial x^2$ |

*(3 marks: 1 mark for definition + 2 marks for comparison with example)*

### Question 2
**[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Supplementary Style]**
**Q: State the Arrhenius equation. Explain the significance of activation energy ($E_a$) in food quality degradation.**

**Model Answer:**

**Arrhenius Equation:**

$$k = A \cdot e^{-E_a / (R T)}$$

**Significance of $E_a$ (Activation Energy):**
- It represents the **minimum energy barrier** that reactant molecules must overcome to undergo degradation.
- A **high $E_a$** (e.g., $E_a > 80$ kJ/mol) means the reaction rate is *very temperature-sensitive* — small temperature changes cause large changes in degradation rate. Typical of vitamin loss, non-enzymatic browning.
- A **low $E_a$** (e.g., $E_a < 40$ kJ/mol) means the reaction is *relatively temperature-insensitive* — diffusion-controlled or microbial growth-like.
- Practical use: Determines the **Q₁₀** (temperature quotient) and helps in designing **accelerated shelf-life tests**.

*(3 marks: 1 mark equation + 2 marks significance with examples)*

---

## 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each – Internal Choice)

### Question A (Choice 1)

**[KTU University Exam – Model Paper 2024, Module 3, 14 Marks]**

**(a)** Derive the integrated form of the first-order rate equation $C(t) = C_0 e^{-kt}$ starting from $\frac{dC}{dt} = -kC$. Discuss its application in predicting **microbial inactivation** and **vitamin retention** during thermal processing of food. **[7 Marks]**

**(b)** The ascorbic acid content in a fruit juice degrades following first-order kinetics. The initial concentration is $C_0 = 50$ mg/100 mL, and the rate constant at 25°C is $k_{25} = 0.043$ day⁻¹. Calculate: **(i)** the time required for the ascorbic acid to drop to 50% of its initial value, and **(ii)** the concentration after 20 days. **[7 Marks]**

#### Model Solution for (a)

**Derivation** (Full steps as derived in Section 3.1, presented in answer sheet):

Starting equation:

$$\frac{dC}{dt} = -kC$$

Separation of variables:

$$\frac{dC}{C} = -k\, dt$$

Integration from $C_0$ to $C$ and $0$ to $t$:

$$\int_{C_0}^{C} \frac{dC}{C} = -k \int_0^t dt$$

$$\ln(C) - \ln(C_0) = -kt$$

$$C(t) = C_0 \, e^{-kt}$$

**Applications:**

- **Microbial Inactivation:** Survivor curve $N(t) = N_0 e^{-kt}$ gives the log reduction $\log(N_0/N) = kt/2.303$. Used to determine $D$-value, sterilization value $F_0$, and pasteurization efficacy.
- **Vitamin Retention:** Quality retention ratio $C/C_0 = e^{-kt}$. Critical for heat-sensitive vitamins (C, thiamine) in pasteurized milk, sterilized milk, canned vegetables, and ready-to-drink beverages. Used to optimize HTST vs. UHT processes.

*(7 marks: 3 for derivation, 2 for microbial application, 2 for vitamin application)*

#### Model Solution for (b)

**Given:**
- $C_0 = 50$ mg/100 mL
- $k = 0.043$ day⁻¹

**(i) Time for 50% reduction (half-life):**

$$t_{1/2} = \frac{\ln(2)}{k} = \frac{0.693}{0.043}$$

$$t_{1/2} = 16.12 \text{ days}$$

**(ii) Concentration after 20 days:**

$$C(20) = 50 \times e^{-0.043 \times 20}$$

$$C(20) = 50 \times e^{-0.86}$$

$$C(20) = 50 \times 0.4232$$

$$C(20) = 21.16 \text{ mg/100 mL}$$

**Result:** The juice retains only ~42.3% of its initial ascorbic acid after 20 days at 25°C.

*(7 marks: 2 for setup, 2 for part i, 2 for part ii, 1 for interpretation)*

### Question B (Choice 2 – Alternative)

**[KTU University Exam – Model Paper 2024, Module 3, 14 Marks]**

**(a)** With a neat sketch, explain the **steps involved in developing a food process model**. Discuss the role of **assumptions** and **boundary conditions** in model building. **[7 Marks]**

**(b)** A bakery product loses moisture during baking following the thin-layer Page model: $MR = e^{-(kt)^n}$, with $k = 0.025$ min⁻¹ and $n = 1.15$. The initial moisture content is $M_0 = 1.20$ kg water/kg dry solid, and the equilibrium moisture content is $M_e = 0.05$ kg water/kg dry solid. Calculate: **(i)** the moisture ratio after 30 minutes, and **(ii)** the moisture content at that instant. **[7 Marks]**

#### Model Solution for (a)

**Seven Steps of Model Development:**

1. **Process Definition** – Clearly state what is being predicted (e.g., moisture loss, microbial kill, color change).
2. **Mechanism Identification** – Identify dominant transport/reaction phenomena (heat, mass, kinetics).
3. **Assumption Statement** – E.g., constant air temperature, one-dimensional diffusion, negligible shrinkage.
4. **Governing Equations** – Write Fick's law, Fourier's law, or rate equations.
5. **Initial & Boundary Conditions** – $C(x, 0) = C_0$; $C(L, t) = C_s$ (surface condition).
6. **Solution** – Analytical or numerical (finite difference, finite element, COMSOL).
7. **Validation** – Compare with experiments (R², RMSE, MAPE).

**Role of Assumptions:** Simplify the mathematics and reduce parameters. *Too many* assumptions → loss of accuracy; *too few* → unsolvable equations.

**Role of Boundary Conditions:** Translate the physical environment (e.g., surface heat transfer coefficient, air humidity) into mathematical constraints that close the problem.

*(7 marks: 4 for the 7-step explanation, 3 for assumptions & BC discussion)*

#### Model Solution for (b)

**Given:**
- $M_0 = 1.20$ kg/kg (db)
- $M_e = 0.05$ kg/kg (db)
- $k = 0.025$ min⁻¹
- $n = 1.15$

**(i) Moisture Ratio after 30 min:**

$$MR = e^{-(kt)^n} = e^{-(0.025 \times 30)^{1.15}}$$

Compute $kt$:

$$kt = 0.025 \times 30 = 0.75$$

Compute $(kt)^{1.15}$:

$$(0.75)^{1.15} = e^{1.15 \times \ln(0.75)} = e^{1.15 \times (-0.2877)} = e^{-0.3308} = 0.7184$$

Therefore:

$$MR = e^{-0.7184} = 0.4876$$

**(ii) Moisture Content at $t = 30$ min:**

Recall:

$$MR = \frac{M - M_e}{M_0 - M_e}$$

Solve for $M$:

$$M = M_e + MR \cdot (M_0 - M_e)$$

$$M = 0.05 + 0.4876 \times (1.20 - 0.05)$$

$$M = 0.05 + 0.4876 \times 1.15$$

$$M = 0.05 + 0.5607$$

$$M = 0.6107 \text{ kg water/kg dry solid}$$

**Result:** The product moisture drops from 1.20 to ~0.61 kg/kg (db) in 30 minutes of baking.

*(7 marks: 3 for MR calculation, 3 for M calculation, 1 for units & interpretation)*

## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Marks-Loss Pitfalls in Module 3 Overview Questions:**
> 1. **Forgetting to convert units** – Temperature must be in **Kelvin** for Arrhenius; $D$-values require consistent time units (min or s).
> 2. **Mixing $MR$ definitions** – Always specify whether $MR$ uses $M_e$ or simply $M/M_0$. KTU expects the **Henderson-Pabis standard** form.
> 3. **Skipping derivation integration steps** – Showing only the final $C = C_0 e^{-kt}$ without the separation and integration steps costs 2–3 marks in Part B.
> 4. **Not stating assumptions** – Any model answer that uses a first-order or Fick's law assumption must explicitly state it (e.g., "isothermal, dilute system, constant diffusivity").
> 5. **Confusing $D$-value and $z$-value** – $D$ = time for 1-log reduction at fixed $T$; $z$ = temperature change for 10-fold $D$ change. Examiners actively test this distinction.

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist — Module 3: Overview of Food Process Modelling & Applications**

**Core Concepts:**
- Food process model = mathematical surrogate of a real food operation.
- Three types: **Empirical, Semi-theoretical, Theoretical** (know one example for each).
- Two spatial treatments: **Lumped** (uniform) and **Distributed** (gradient).

**Foundational Equations (must memorize & derive):**
- First-order decay: $C(t) = C_0 e^{-kt}$
- Arrhenius: $k = A \exp[-E_a/(RT)]$ and its two-point form
- Fick's 2nd law: $\partial C/\partial t = D \cdot \partial^2 C/\partial x^2$
- Fourier's 2nd law: $\partial T/\partial t = \alpha \cdot \partial^2 T/\partial x^2$
- Page drying: $MR = e^{-(kt)^n}$
- $D$-value relation: $D_{ref} = D_T \cdot 10^{(T_{ref} - T)/z}$

**Key Parameters & Units:**
- $E_a$ (J/mol or kJ/mol), $R = 8.314$ J/(mol·K), $T$ (K)
- $D_{eff}$ (m²/s), $\alpha$ (m²/s), $k$ (1/s or 1/h)
- $z$-value (°C), $D$-value (min or s)
- $a_w$ (dimensionless, 0–1)

**Major Applications:**
- Drying (Page, Lewis, Henderson-Pabis, Fick)
- Thermal processing (Arrhenius + $F_0$ calculation)
- Shelf-life prediction (ASLT, Q₁₀, $z$-model)
- Freezing (Plank's equation, Fourier heat conduction)
- Microbial growth (Monod, Logistic, Gompertz)

**Modelling Workflow:** Define → Identify mechanisms → Assumptions → Equations → IC & BC → Solve → Validate.

**Validation Metrics:** $R^2 \geq 0.90$, low RMSE, low $\chi^2$, random residual distribution.

**Industrial Significance:** Reduces R&D cost, enables scale-up, ensures safety (sterilization, HACCP), supports digital twins and Industry 4.0 food factories.

topics.title: Types of Models and Their Use in Food Processing
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEFTT521 - MODELLING & SIMULATION IN FOOD PROCESSING
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Types of Models and Their Use in Food Processing

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# Section 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

A **model** in the context of food process engineering is a mathematical, physical, or logical representation of a real food system, process, or phenomenon. It is constructed by abstracting the essential features of a complex food process into a set of equations, logical rules, or physical replicas that can be analyzed, solved, and used for prediction, design, optimization, and control.

In the KTU 2024 Scheme framework (NEP 2020 aligned), **modelling and simulation** refers to the systematic use of these representations to study the behavior of food processing operations (such as drying, extrusion, pasteurization, fermentation, and storage) without performing exhaustive, costly, or time-consuming physical experiments.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Syllabus Highlight (Module 3):**
> Students must be able to:
> 1. Classify the different categories of models used in food processing.
> 2. Identify the appropriate model class for a given food process.
> 3. Justify the engineering utility (design, control, optimization, safety) of each model type.

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — The "Food Process Map"

Imagine a chef who wants to bake the perfect loaf of bread every single time. Instead of baking 1000 loaves and noting the outcomes, the chef creates a **mental recipe-map** that says:

- If oven temperature is $T$ and baking time is $t$, the crust color $C$ will follow a certain pattern.
- If the dough has moisture content $M$, the crumb softness $S$ will behave predictably.

This "recipe-map" is exactly what a **process model** is for a food engineer. It condenses thousands of experiments into a single, useful, predictive equation. The simulation is then the act of "running" that map on a computer to forecast outcomes under new conditions — without lighting the oven.

## 1.3 The Two Pillars of a Food Process Model

> [!NOTE]
> **Core Definition — Model vs. Simulation:**
> - **Model** = the *equation*, the *diagram*, or the *logical structure* that describes the system.
> - **Simulation** = the act of *solving* the model numerically or analytically to obtain the system's response under specified conditions.

A **food process model** is fundamentally a translation of real phenomena (heat transfer, moisture loss, microbial growth, structural change) into a *language of mathematics*. This allows engineers to:
- Predict product quality **before** manufacturing.
- Scale up from a lab process to an industrial line.
- Save raw materials, energy, and time.
- Ensure food safety via microbial and shelf-life models.

## 1.4 Standard Metrics Every Food Model Must Carry

Every credible food processing model must incorporate these standard metrics:

- **Temperature** ($T$, typically in **K** or **°C**)
- **Time** ($t$, in **s**, **min**, or **h**)
- **Moisture content** ($M$, in **% w.b.** or **% d.b.**)
- **Water activity** ($a_w$, dimensionless, $0 \le a_w \le 1$)
- **Concentration** ($C$, in **kg/m³** or **mol/m³**)
- **Thermal conductivity** ($k$, in **W/(m·K)**)
- **Mass diffusivity** ($D$, in **m²/s**)
- **Reaction rate constant** ($k$, in **1/s** or **1/min**)

## 1.5 Why This Topic Matters in Food Engineering

The food industry is unique because:
- Raw materials are **biological** and therefore **variable** (no two tomatoes are identical).
- Products are **perishable**, so time and temperature must be tightly controlled.
- Quality attributes (texture, color, flavor, nutrition) are **multi-dimensional** and interlinked.

A model is the only systematic way to manage this variability while ensuring **safety, quality, and efficiency** simultaneously.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# Section 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Hierarchical Classification of Models Used in Food Processing

Food process models can be classified along **multiple independent axes**. A single real-world food process (say, hot-air drying of apple slices) may be described by *several* of these model types simultaneously, and a deep understanding requires familiarity with **all** of them.

### Axis A — By Mathematical / Physical Basis

1. **Empirical (Black-Box) Models**
   - Built purely from experimental data using curve-fitting.
   - No physical insight; only "input → output" mapping.
   - *Example:* correlating drying rate with temperature via a simple exponential fit.
   - **Advantage:** fast to develop. **Disadvantage:** valid only inside the experimental range.

2. **Theoretical / Fundamental (White-Box) Models**
   - Derived from first principles of physics, chemistry, or biology (mass, energy, momentum balances).
   - *Example:* solving Fourier's heat conduction equation inside a potato during baking.
   - **Advantage:** physically meaningful, broadly extrapolatable. **Disadvantage:** requires many parameters.

3. **Semi-Theoretical (Grey-Box) Models**
   - Hybrid: a theoretical skeleton fitted with empirical constants.
   - *Example:* the Page drying model, the Henderson-Pabis model, the Fickian diffusion model with an *effective* diffusivity.
   - This is the **most common** model type in real food engineering practice.

### Axis B — By Time Dependence

4. **Steady-State Models** — system properties do not change with time ($\partial/\partial t = 0$). Used for continuous processes at equilibrium (e.g., steady-state pasteurization in a plate heat exchanger).

5. **Dynamic (Transient) Models** — system properties evolve with time ($\partial/\partial t \neq 0$). Mandatory for batch processes such as drying, baking, and fermentation.

### Axis C — By Spatial Description

6. **Lumped Parameter Models** — assume uniform property inside the food (no spatial gradients). Valid when **Biot Number** $Bi < 0.1$.
   *Example:* small particles, well-mixed liquids.*

7. **Distributed Parameter Models** — account for spatial variation of temperature, moisture, etc. Require partial differential equations (PDEs).
   *Example:* temperature profile inside a meat loaf during cooking.*

### Axis D — By Uncertainty

8. **Deterministic Models** — given inputs produce a single, exact output. Most engineering models fall here.
   *Example:* Newton's law of cooling.*

9. **Stochastic / Probabilistic Models** — outputs are described by probability distributions.
   *Example:* microbial growth models, shelf-life stochastic simulations, Monte Carlo food safety models.*

### Axis E — By System Continuity

10. **Continuous Models** — variables vary continuously with time/space (differential equations).
11. **Discrete Event Models** — system changes at distinct events (e.g., a packaging line simulation, batch scheduling).

## 2.2 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The following table contains the **core equations** the KTU 2024 examiner expects you to recognize and apply. Memorize the form, the variable meanings, and the units.

**Table 2.1 — Master Formula Cheat Sheet for Food Process Models**

| Equation Name | Mathematical Form | Meaning of Terms | Typical Use in Food Processing |
|---|---|---|---|
| Fourier's Law (Heat Conduction) | $q = -k \dfrac{dT}{dx}$ | $q$ = heat flux [W/m²], $k$ = thermal conductivity [W/(m·K)] | Baking, frying, pasteurization |
| Newton's Law of Cooling | $\dfrac{dT}{dt} = -h \dfrac{A}{m c_p} (T - T_\infty)$ | $h$ = heat transfer coefficient [W/(m²·K)] | Cooling of cooked foods, refrigeration |
| Fick's First Law of Diffusion | $J = -D \dfrac{dC}{dx}$ | $J$ = mass flux [kg/(m²·s)], $D$ = diffusivity [m²/s] | Moisture migration, salt diffusion, sugar infusion |
| Fick's Second Law (1D slab) | $\dfrac{\partial C}{\partial t} = D \dfrac{\partial^2 C}{\partial x^2}$ | — | Drying, rehydration, leaching |
| Arrhenius Equation | $k = k_0 \, e^{-E_a / (R T)}$ | $k_0$ = pre-exponential, $E_a$ = activation energy [J/mol], $R$ = 8.314 J/(mol·K) | Quality degradation, nutrient loss, enzyme inactivation |
| Zero-Order Reaction | $C = C_0 - k t$ | — | Slow quality loss at low $a_w$ |
| First-Order Reaction | $C = C_0 \, e^{-k t}$ | — | Vitamin degradation, color fading |
| Page Drying Model | $MR = e^{-k t^n}$ | $MR$ = moisture ratio, $k$, $n$ = empirical constants | Thin-layer drying of fruits/vegetables |
| Henderson-Pabis Model | $MR = a \, e^{-k t}$ | $a$ = empirical constant | Drying of agricultural products |
| Midilli-Kuc drying model | $MR = a \, e^{-k t^n} + b t$ | — | Most accurate fit for many foods |
| Logistic Microbial Growth | $N(t) = \dfrac{N_{max}}{1 + \left(\dfrac{N_{max} - N_0}{N_0}\right) e^{-\mu_{max} t}}$ | $\mu_{max}$ = max specific growth rate [1/h] | Predicting spoilage organism growth |
| Modified Gompertz Model | $\log N = A + C \, e^{-e^{-B(t - M)}}$ | $A, B, C, M$ = Gompertz parameters | Microbial growth curve fitting |
| Bigelow / D-value model | $\log \dfrac{N}{N_0} = -\dfrac{t}{D_T}$ | $D_T$ = decimal reduction time at temp $T$ | Thermal sterilization, pasteurization |
| z-value model | $\log \dfrac{D_{T_1}}{D_{T_2}} = \dfrac{T_2 - T_1}{z}$ | $z$ = temperature change for 10-fold change in D | Thermal process design |
| F0 value (sterilization value) | $F_0 = \int 10^{(T(t) - T_{ref})/z} \, dt$ | $T_{ref}$ = 121.1 °C, $z$ = 10 °C | Canned food sterilization |
| Reynolds Number | $Re = \dfrac{\rho v L}{\mu}$ | $\rho$ = density, $v$ = velocity, $L$ = char. length, $\mu$ = viscosity | Flow regime (laminar/turbulent) in pipes, heat exchangers |
| Prandtl Number | $Pr = \dfrac{c_p \mu}{k}$ | $c_p$ = specific heat | Relative thickness of velocity and thermal boundary layers |
| Biot Number | $Bi = \dfrac{h L_c}{k}$ | $L_c$ = characteristic length | Lumped vs. distributed system check |
| Fourier Number (Thermal) | $Fo = \dfrac{\alpha t}{L^2}$ | $\alpha = k/(\rho c_p)$ | Dimensionless time for unsteady heat conduction |
| Damköhler Number | $Da = \dfrac{k L}{v}$ | — | Reaction vs. transport rate in food reactors |
| Weibull Survival Model | $\log N = \log N_0 - \left(\dfrac{t}{\delta}\right)^p$ | $\delta$ = scale, $p$ = shape parameter | Non-linear microbial inactivation |

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Valuation Tip:** Always write the equation first, **then define every symbol with units**. Examiners award 1 mark for the formula and 1 mark for correct symbol definition in most 14-mark derivations.

## 2.3 Engineering Utility of These Models

- **Design** — sizing of a heat exchanger, dryer, or sterilizer.
- **Optimization** — finding the *optimal* time–temperature combination for a baked product.
- **Control** — building a feedback loop in a pasteurizer using a dynamic temperature model.
- **Safety** — ensuring a thermal process delivers $F_0 \ge 3$ min for low-acid canned foods.
- **Shelf-life prediction** — combining Arrhenius kinetics with critical quality limits.
- **Scale-up** — using dimensionless numbers ($Re$, $Pr$, $Bi$, $Fo$) to translate a lab process to industrial scale.
- **Process troubleshooting** — identifying bottlenecks via mass/energy balance models.

## 2.4 Selecting the Right Model — A Decision Logic

> [!NOTE]
> **Step-by-step model selection logic (commonly tested in KTU 14-mark questions):**
> 1. **Identify the dominant phenomenon** — heat transfer, mass transfer, reaction, or microbial growth.
> 2. **Check the spatial scale** — small or large? Compute $Bi$. If $Bi < 0.1$ → lumped; else distributed.
> 3. **Check the time scale** — steady or transient? If inputs change → dynamic.
> 4. **Check data availability** — only experimental data? Use empirical. Have physical parameters? Use theoretical.
> 5. **Validate the model** — compare predictions with independent experimental data using statistical indices (RMSE, $R^2$, MAPE).
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# Section 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation 1 — Lumped Parameter Unsteady Heat Transfer Model (Newton's Law of Cooling)

This is the **single most frequently asked derivation** in KTU examinations on this topic. It is the foundation of cooling, freezing, and pasteurization models.

### Step 1 — Energy Balance on a Food Particle

Consider a food body of mass $m$, specific heat $c_p$, surface area $A$, and uniform temperature $T(t)$ at time $t$, placed in an environment at temperature $T_\infty$ with convective heat transfer coefficient $h$.

The rate of energy loss by convection equals the rate of decrease of internal energy:

$$
-m \, c_p \, \dfrac{dT}{dt} = h \, A \, (T - T_\infty)
$$

> **Logic row:** The negative sign on the left indicates that as the food cools, $T$ falls with time, so $dT/dt$ is negative, and the right side (positive when $T > T_\infty$) represents heat leaving the body. The sign convention ensures energy conservation.

### Step 2 — Rearranging into Standard Form

$$
\dfrac{dT}{dt} = -\dfrac{h A}{m c_p} (T - T_\infty)
$$

### Step 3 — Define the Time Constant

Introduce the **time constant** $\tau$ as:

$$
\tau = \dfrac{m c_p}{h A} \quad \text{[seconds]}
$$

The governing equation becomes:

$$
\dfrac{dT}{dt} = -\dfrac{1}{\tau} (T - T_\infty)
$$

### Step 4 — Separation of Variables

Let $\theta = T - T_\infty$ (the excess temperature). Then $d\theta = dT$ and:

$$
\dfrac{d\theta}{dt} = -\dfrac{\theta}{\tau}
$$

Separate:

$$
\dfrac{d\theta}{\theta} = -\dfrac{dt}{\tau}
$$

### Step 5 — Integration with Initial Condition

Integrate from $t = 0$ (when $\theta = \theta_0 = T_0 - T_\infty$) to $t$:

$$
\int_{\theta_0}^{\theta} \dfrac{d\theta}{\theta} = -\dfrac{1}{\tau} \int_{0}^{t} dt
$$

$$
\ln \left( \dfrac{\theta}{\theta_0} \right) = -\dfrac{t}{\tau}
$$

### Step 6 — Final Closed-Form Solution

Exponentiate:

$$
\dfrac{T(t) - T_\infty}{T_0 - T_\infty} = e^{-t / \tau}
$$

$$
\boxed{\,T(t) = T_\infty + (T_0 - T_\infty) \, e^{-t / \tau}\,}
$$

> **Logic row:** This is the iconic **Newtonian cooling law**. It tells us that a food cools exponentially toward $T_\infty$. At $t = \tau$, the temperature has completed $\approx 63.2\%$ of its total possible drop. At $t = 5\tau$, cooling is more than $99\%$ complete — a useful "rule of thumb" in process design.

### Step 7 — Validity Condition

The lumped model is valid **only if** the **Biot number** is small:

$$
Bi = \dfrac{h L_c}{k} < 0.1
$$

where $L_c = V/A$ is the characteristic length. If $Bi \ge 0.1$, internal temperature gradients dominate and a distributed parameter model is required.

## 3.2 Derivation 2 — One-Dimensional Unsteady Moisture Diffusion (Fickian Drying Model)

This derivation underpins almost every food drying model.

### Step 1 — Mass Balance in a Differential Slab Element

Consider a thin slab of thickness $dx$ in a food piece. The moisture enters by diffusion at $x$ and leaves at $x + dx$:

$$
\dfrac{\partial C}{\partial t} = -\dfrac{\partial J}{\partial x}
$$

### Step 2 — Substitute Fick's First Law

With $J = -D \, \partial C / \partial x$:

$$
\dfrac{\partial C}{\partial t} = -\dfrac{\partial}{\partial x}\left(-D \dfrac{\partial C}{\partial x}\right) = D \dfrac{\partial^2 C}{\partial x^2}
$$

> **Logic row:** This is **Fick's Second Law of Diffusion**, the moisture analog of the heat equation. It assumes a constant effective diffusivity $D$, isotropic medium, and 1D transport.

### Step 3 — Boundary & Initial Conditions for a Slab

$$
\begin{aligned}
C(x, 0) &= C_0 \quad \text{(uniform initial moisture)} \\
C(0, t) &= C_s \quad \text{(symmetry at center)} \\
-D \dfrac{\partial C}{\partial x}\bigg|_{x=L} &= h_m \, (C(L,t) - C_\infty) \quad \text{(surface mass transfer)}
\end{aligned}
$$

### Step 4 — Define the Moisture Ratio

$$
MR(t) = \dfrac{\bar{C}(t) - C_{eq}}{C_0 - C_{eq}}
$$

where $\bar{C}(t)$ is the spatial average moisture, $C_{eq}$ is the equilibrium moisture content, and $C_0$ is the initial.

### Step 5 — Series Solution (Crank, 1975)

The analytical solution for a slab, for small surface resistance, is:

$$
MR = \dfrac{8}{\pi^2} \sum_{n=0}^{\infty} \dfrac{1}{(2n+1)^2} \exp\left[- (2n+1)^2 \dfrac{\pi^2 D t}{4 L^2}\right]
$$

For long times (the so-called *regular regime*), only the first term is significant:

$$
\boxed{\,MR \approx \dfrac{8}{\pi^2} \exp\left[- \dfrac{\pi^2 D t}{4 L^2}\right]\,}
$$

> **Logic row:** This single exponential decay form is the theoretical backbone that justifies using a Henderson-Pabis or Page model. The parameter $k$ in those empirical models is therefore not arbitrary — it equals $\pi^2 D / (4 L^2)$ when the regular regime holds.

## 3.3 Derivation 3 — Arrhenius-Based Shelf-Life Model

### Step 1 — Quality Degradation as a First-Order Reaction

For many quality attributes $Q$ (vitamin retention, color intensity, texture firmness):

$$
\dfrac{dQ}{dt} = -k \, Q \quad \Rightarrow \quad Q(t) = Q_0 \, e^{-k t}
$$

### Step 2 — Temperature Dependence of $k$ via Arrhenius

$$
k = k_0 \, \exp\left(-\dfrac{E_a}{R T}\right)
$$

where $T$ is in **kelvin** (note: NOT Celsius in the exponential — a very common KTU pitfall).

### Step 3 — Combine Both Equations

$$
Q(t, T) = Q_0 \, \exp\left[-k_0 \, t \, e^{-E_a / (R T)}\right]
$$

### Step 4 — Define the End of Shelf Life

Shelf life $t_s$ is the time at which $Q$ reaches a critical value $Q_c$:

$$
t_s = -\dfrac{1}{k_0} \, e^{E_a / (R T)} \, \ln\left( \dfrac{Q_c}{Q_0} \right)
$$

$$
\boxed{\,\ln t_s = \ln\left(-\dfrac{1}{k_0} \ln \dfrac{Q_c}{Q_0}\right) + \dfrac{E_a}{R} \cdot \dfrac{1}{T}\,}
$$

> **Logic row:** Plotting $\ln t_s$ versus $1/T$ (Kelvin) gives a **straight line** with slope $E_a / R$. This is the *accelerated shelf-life testing* principle: store food at elevated temperatures, measure quality loss rapidly, then linearly extrapolate to real storage temperature.

## 3.4 Analytical Matrix — When to Use Which Model

> [!NOTE]
> **Quick-reference decision matrix** (memorize for KTU Part A questions):

| Food Process | Dominant Phenomenon | Recommended Model | Key Equation |
|---|---|---|---|
| Pasteurization of milk | Heat transfer, microbial inactivation | Lumped thermal + Bigelow | $F_0$ value |
| Hot-air drying of mango | Mass transfer (moisture diffusion) | Fickian slab + Page empirical | $MR = e^{-kt^n}$ |
| Baking of bread | Coupled heat & mass transfer with reaction | Distributed PDE + Arrhenius | $\partial T/\partial t = \alpha \partial^2 T/\partial x^2$ |
| Freezing of fish | Heat transfer with phase change | Plank's equation / lumped | $t_f = (\rho L / \theta)(PL/2k + Rh_c)$ |
| Fermentation of wine | Microbial kinetics | Logistic / Monod | $N(t) = N_{max}/(1 + (N_{max}/N_0 - 1)e^{-\mu t})$ |
| Extrusion cooking | Rheology, reaction, heat | Navier–Stokes + Arrhenius | $P = \eta \dot{\gamma}^n$ (power-law fluid) |
| Salt curing of meat | Mass transfer (osmotic diffusion) | Fickian + empirical Peleg | $MR = 1 - k_1 t/(1 + k_2 t)$ |
| Cold-chain storage | Arrhenius quality decay | First-order + Arrhenius | $\ln t_s = A + E_a/(RT)$ |
| Irradiation of spices | Microbial inactivation | Weibull survival | $\log N = \log N_0 - (t/\delta)^p$ |

## 3.5 Worked Numerical Example (KTU Practice)

**Problem (typical 7-mark sub-part):**

A spherical fruit of diameter $0.05$ m, density $1000$ kg/m³, and specific heat $3800$ J/(kg·K) is cooled from $25$ °C to $5$ °C in air at $T_\infty = 0$ °C with $h = 25$ W/(m²·K) and $k_{fruit} = 0.5$ W/(m·K). Check whether a lumped model is valid, and find the time required to reach $5$ °C.

**Solution:**

For a sphere, $L_c = r/3 = (0.025)/3 = 0.00833$ m.

$$
Bi = \dfrac{h \, L_c}{k} = \dfrac{25 \times 0.00833}{0.5} = 0.416
$$

Since $Bi > 0.1$, the lumped model is **NOT strictly valid**, but we proceed to illustrate (the model is *approximately* applicable for a quick estimate).

Surface area: $A = \pi d^2 = \pi (0.05)^2 = 7.854 \times 10^{-3}$ m².

Volume: $V = \dfrac{4}{3} \pi r^3 = \dfrac{4}{3} \pi (0.025)^3 = 6.545 \times 10^{-5}$ m³.

Mass: $m = \rho V = 1000 \times 6.545 \times 10^{-5} = 0.06545$ kg.

Time constant:

$$
\tau = \dfrac{m c_p}{h A} = \dfrac{0.06545 \times 3800}{25 \times 7.854 \times 10^{-3}} = 1267 \text{ s} \approx 21.1 \text{ min}
$$

Using the cooling law:

$$
\dfrac{T(t) - T_\infty}{T_0 - T_\infty} = e^{-t/\tau}
$$

$$
\dfrac{5 - 0}{25 - 0} = e^{-t / 1267}
$$

$$
0.2 = e^{-t/1267}
$$

$$
t = -1267 \cdot \ln(0.2) = 1267 \times 1.609 = 2039 \text{ s} \approx 34 \text{ min}
$$

**Answer:** The fruit reaches 5 °C in approximately **34 minutes** under the lumped assumption. A more accurate distributed-parameter model would yield a slightly longer time, because the center cools slower than the lumped assumption predicts.
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

# Section 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Mermaid Flowchart — Hierarchical Classification of Food Process Models

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    nodeA[Food Process Models]
    nodeB[By Mathematical Basis]
    nodeC[By Time Dependence]
    nodeD[By Spatial Description]
    nodeE[By Uncertainty]
    nodeF[Empirical Black Box]
    nodeG[Semi Theoretical Grey Box]
    nodeH[Theoretical White Box]
    nodeI[Steady State Models]
    nodeJ[Dynamic Transient Models]
    nodeK[Lumped Parameter]
    nodeL[Distributed Parameter]
    nodeM[Deterministic Models]
    nodeN[Stochastic Models]
    nodeO[Uses in Food Processing]
    nodeP[Drying Modeling]
    nodeQ[Heat Transfer Modeling]
    nodeR[Microbial Growth Prediction]
    nodeS[Shelf Life Estimation]
    nodeT[Process Optimization]
    nodeU[Quality and Safety Control]

    nodeA --> nodeB
    nodeA --> nodeC
    nodeA --> nodeD
    nodeA --> nodeE
    nodeA --> nodeO

    nodeB --> nodeF
    nodeB --> nodeG
    nodeB --> nodeH

    nodeC --> nodeI
    nodeC --> nodeJ

    nodeD --> nodeK
    nodeD --> nodeL

    nodeE --> nodeM
    nodeE --> nodeN

    nodeO --> nodeP
    nodeO --> nodeQ
    nodeO --> nodeR
    nodeO --> nodeS
    nodeO --> nodeT
    nodeO --> nodeU
```

## 4.2 Mermaid Block Diagram — Modeling Workflow in Food Process Engineering

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    stepA[Real Food Process] --> stepB[Define System Boundaries]
    stepB --> stepC[Identify Dominant Phenomenon]
    stepC --> stepD{Physical Parameters Available?}
    stepD -- Yes --> stepE[Build Theoretical Model]
    stepD -- No --> stepF[Collect Experimental Data]
    stepF --> stepG[Fit Empirical Model]
    stepE --> stepH[Solve Equations Analytically or Numerically]
    stepG --> stepH
    stepH --> stepI[Validate Against Independent Data]
    stepI --> stepJ{Model Acceptable?}
    stepJ -- No --> stepK[Refine or Change Model Class]
    stepK --> stepC
    stepJ -- Yes --> stepL[Use for Design, Optimization, and Control]
    stepL --> stepM[Industrial Implementation]
```

## 4.3 Mermaid Schematic — Drying Model Architecture (Thin-Layer Drying)

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    in1[Input Air Temperature T_in] --> dry1[Drying Chamber]
    in2[Input Air Velocity v] --> dry1
    in3[Initial Moisture M_0] --> dry1
    in4[Initial Temperature T_0] --> dry1

    dry1 --> dry2[Governing Equations]
    dry2 --> eq1[Energy Balance on Solid]
    dry2 --> eq2[Mass Balance of Moisture]
    dry2 --> eq3[Energy Balance on Air]
    dry2 --> eq4[Rate of Moisture Removal]

    eq1 --> out1[Transient Temperature Profile T x t]
    eq2 --> out2[Moisture Content M x t]
    eq3 --> out3[Air Outlet Temperature]
    eq4 --> out4[Drying Rate Curve]

    out2 --> fm1[Fit Empirical Models]
    fm1 --> fm2[Page Model]
    fm1 --> fm3[Henderson Pabis]
    fm1 --> fm4[Midilli Kuc]
    fm1 --> fm5[Logarithmic Model]
```

## 4.4 Mermaid Block Diagram — Microbial Inactivation / Growth Model Framework

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    start[Microbial Population at t0] --> cond{Effect of Process}
    cond -- Growth --> grow[Gompertz or Logistic Model]
    cond -- Inactivation --> kill[Bigelow D value or Weibull Model]

    grow --> out1[Predictive Microbiology]
    kill --> out2[Thermal Process Design]

    out1 --> app1[Shelf Life Estimation]
    out1 --> app2[Refrigeration Design]
    out2 --> app3[Sterilization F0 Calculation]
    out2 --> app4[Pasteurization Validation]
    out2 --> app5[Non Thermal Process Evaluation]
```

## 4.5 Block-Level Functional Architecture — Why Each Model Type is Used

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    core[Food Process Modelling] --> layer1[Layer 1 Physical Understanding]
    core --> layer2[Layer 2 Mathematical Translation]
    core --> layer3[Layer 3 Computational Solution]
    core --> layer4[Layer 4 Engineering Application]

    layer1 --> l1a[Heat Mass Momentum Balances]
    layer1 --> l1b[Microbial Kinetics]
    layer1 --> l1c[Quality Degradation Chemistry]

    layer2 --> l2a[ODEs and PDEs]
    layer2 --> l2b[Empirical Correlations]
    layer2 --> l2c[Statistical Distributions]

    layer3 --> l3a[Analytical Solutions]
    l3a --> l3a1[Crank Solution for Diffusion]
    l3a --> l3a2[Newtonian Cooling Exponential]
    layer3 --> l3b[Numerical Methods]
    l3b --> l3b1[Finite Difference]
    l3b --> l3b2[Finite Element]
    l3b --> l3b3[Computational Fluid Dynamics]

    layer4 --> l4a[Design of Equipment]
    layer4 --> l4b[Process Control Loops]
    layer4 --> l4c[Quality and Safety Assurance]
    layer4 --> l4d[Scale Up and Optimization]
```
